Tuesday,  March  20,  1984 


Studying  in  the  cafeteria? 
Try  the  student  lounge! 


.  In  the  SPOTLIGHT'S  opinion 


Cafeteria  (kaf  e  tir  e  a)  n: 
a  restaurant  in  which  the 
customers  serve  themselves 
or  are  served  at  a  counter  and 
take  the  food  to  tables  to  eat. 
This  is  how  Webster's 
New  Collegiate  Dictionary 
delines  the  word.  But  no 
where  under  the  word 
cafeteria  does  Ivlerriam- 
Webster  list  socializing  or  stu- 
dying with  books,  purses, 
coats,  and  backpacks  strewn 
over  the  tables  and  chairs 
which  were  originally  intended 
for  diners. 

Serving  students,  faculty, 
staff,  and  visitors,  the  cafeteria 
has  ample  space  to  accomm- 
date  the  lunch  crowd  at  noon- 
time, providing  that  "space"  is 
not  selfishly  taken  up  by  ob- 
jects not  really  needing  the 
comforts  of  chairs. 

Why  should  people  pay- 
ing for  cafeteria  food  and  ser- 
vices be  forced  to  stand  in 
bewilderment  and  juggle  a 
trayful  of  food  while  searching 
for  a  place  to  sil  down  and  eat 
their  meal? 

The  student  lounge  in  the 
Academic  Center  is  furnished 
with  lounge  furniture,  study 
tables  and  comlortable 
carpeting,  certainly  a  preferred 
place  of  concentration  -  a 
more  appropriate  place  for 
scattering  notebooks  and  tex- 
tbooks. 

Since  the  cafeteria  is  a 
place  to  eat,  and  not  to 
socialize  or  study,  we  could 
have  more  consideration  for 
those  who  simply  want  to  eat 
their  lunch.

want  a  Big  Mac  and  large  fries  given  to  those  who  promise  to  vote  for 
me."

SCHOLARSHIPS  AVAILABLE 

For  1984-85,  the  College  will  be  awarding  $500  scholarships  lo  approiimalely  14  returning  sindenis  who 
have  a  cumulative  grade  point  average  of  3.0  or  higher. 

Interested  students  must  complete  the  scholarship  appUcation  and  include  at  least  two  letten  of  support 
from  Iheir  division  directors  or  their  instructors. 

The  deadline  to  apply  is  April  1,  1984.  Applications  are  available  at  the  circulation  desk  in  the  Library  in 
the  Learning  Resources  Center  and  in  the  Financial  Aid  Office,  Room  201,  Academic  Center.

Tuesday,  March  20,  1984     â€˘     SPOTLIGHT 


College  budget  released; 

shows  smallest  rate  of  increase 

over  last  three  years 


The  College  has  unveiled  a  proposed  $16.7 
million  budget  for  1984-85.  Copies  of  the  docu- 
ment were  released  to  the  members  of  the  20 
-member  College  Executive  Council  on  Friday, 
March  9. 

According  to  Dr.  Robert  L.  Breuder,  Col- 
lege president,  the  proposed  budget  shows  the 
smallest  rate  of  increase  -  7.9  percent  overall, 
7.5  percent  main  campus  -  of  any  over  the  last 
three  years. 

"The  cost  to  the  sponsor  districts  per  EFT 
(equivalent  full-time)  student  is  actually  down 
5.1  percent  from  that  approved  by  the  districts 
for  1983-84,"  Dr.  Breuder  stated  in  releasing  the 
budget.  "It  is  up  only  3.5  percent  above  the  per 
EFT  cost  of  last  year  (1982-83),"  he  continued. 

Nearly  all  of  the  increased  total  is  said  to  be 
attributable  to  growth  in  enrollment  (2  percent 
up  over  this  year);  costs  associated  with  start-up 
and  maintenance  of  equipment  in  new  programs 
and  programs  to  be  enhanced  with  new  advanced 
technology,  and  to  inflation,  employee  benefits 
and  salaries,  and  increased  utility  service  to  new 
facilities. 

"We  are  extremely  sensitive  to  the  need  to 
limit  increases  in  costs  to  our  sponsors, "Dr. 
Breuder  said.  "This  budget  reflects  our  efforts 
to  hold  down  sponsor  costs  without  sacrificing 
excellence  or  limiting  opportunities." 

The  proposed  $16.7  million  budget  includes 
$13  million  for  postsecondary  operating  ex- 
penses, of  which  $12.64  million  is  proposed  for 
main  campus  postsecondary  programs  and  $0.36 
million  for  the  North  Campus.  Sponsors  would 
pay  $2.4  million  (16.5  percent;  students,  $6.1 
million  (42.7  percent),  and  the  state,  $3.8 
million  (26.8  percent)  for  operating. 

The  $12.64  million  proposed  for  main  cam- 
pus postsecondary  operating  represents  an  in- 
crease of  9  percent  over  1983-84,  with  94  percent 
of  that  increase  deriving  from  increased  costs  in 
four  categories:  computer  software,  rate  increase 
in  employee  benefits  and  salaries,  preventive 
maintenance  for  computer  software,  and  utilities 
-  reflecting  both  service  to  new  building  and  in- 
structional space  not  in  service  in  1983-84  and 
rate  increases. 


Much  of  the  increased  cost  in  computer 
software  and  maintenance  relates  to  four  new  ad- 
vanced technology  laboratories,  utilizing  com- 
puter enhanced  systems,  to  be  established  next 
year;  computer  assisted  design  and  manufactur- 
ing, metrology,  computerized  numerical  control, 
and  programmable  logic  control.  Additional 
new  initiatives  influencing  costs  to  some  extent 
include  new  programs  in  human  services  and 
quantity  foods. 

Operating  increases  for  the  North  Campus 

-  now  entering  its  second  full  year  of  operation 

-  will  total  $0.1  million.  That  increase  primarily 
reflects  increased  enrollments  brought  about  by 
the  opening  of  two  new  programs  in  accounting 
and  business  management,  and  by  the  addition 
of  second-year  students.  Dr.  Breuder  noted  that 
the  North  Campus  is  self-sustaining,  requiring 
no  subsidies  from  the  Williamsport  operation. 
In  fact,  the  operation  of  the  North  Campus  is  ex- 
pected -  for  the  second  consecutive  year  ~  to 
reduce  the  per  EFT  costs  of  the  College. 

Employee  benefits  and  salaries  now  account 
for  79  percent  of  all  postsecondary  operating 
costs,  and  despite  the  College's  efforts  to  remedy 
inequities,  data  acquired  during  the  1983-84 
school  year  reveals  that  the  College  remunera- 
tion scales  continue  to  lag  behind  those  of  com- 
parable employers  for  all  categories  of 
employees,  said  College  officials. 

The  postsecondary  budget  assumes  an 
enrollment  increase  of  2  percent  in  1984-85. 
Postseconary  enrollment  has  increased  30.7  per- 
cent since  1977-78,  an  average  of  4.4  percent  per 
year. 

The  College's  proposed  postsecondary 
capital  budget  totals  $2.2  million,  a  decrease  of 
1.2  percent  from  the  current  year  figure.  Spon- 
sor districts  would  pay  $0.3  million  (14  percent) 
of  that  total,  with  the  remaining  86  percent  of 
that  amount  coming  fiom  a  variety  of  sources, 
including  state,  private,  non-sponsor  student  and 
federal  sources. 

College  officials  noted  that  capital  projects 
either  completed  or  now  on  the  drawing  board 
for  the  period  1978  to  1986  total  $35  million. 
please  turn  to  page  8 


8     â€˘    SPOTLIGHT    â€˘    Tuesday,  March  20,  1984 


College  budget  released 

fiom  page  7 


The  sponsor  districts'  share  of  that  total  is  $1.3 
milhon  (3.7  percent). 

A  total  of  $1.5  million  is  proposed  for  the 
secondary  (high  school)  component,  including 
$1.4  million  for  operating  and  $0.1  million  for 
capital.  The  proposed  budget  shows  an  increase 
of  only  3  percent,  or  $65  per  student. 

Secondary  enrollment,  which  has  been  a 
concern  in  the  past,  is  expected  to  increase  for 
the  first  time  in  several  years  despite  an  expected 
continued  drop  in  the  numbers  of  secondary 
students  enrolled  in  participating  districts. 

According  to  Dr.  Breuder,  that  projected 
increase  is  the  result  of  both  unprecedented  ef- 
forts to  strengthen  and  diversify  secondary  pro- 
gramming, and  enhanced  recruitment  activity. 

The  College  has  planned  two  secondary 
programs  at  the  North  Campus  --  in  computer 
science  and  health  occupations.  Four  new  senior- 
only  programs  are  planned  at  the  Williamsport 
campus:  dairy  herd  management,  agribusiness, 
computer  operator,  and  computer  science 
technology. 

Dr.  Breuder  expressed  his  satisfaction  that 
the  proposed  minimal  level  of  increase  will  not 
jeopardize  the  College's  efforts  to  expand  and 
modify  programming  to  meet  the  emerging  needs 
of  area  business  and  industry  and  other  College 
constituencies. 

"Limited  cost  increases  does  not  mean  we 
have  to  limit  either  our  services  or  our  quality," 
he  noted.  Dr.  Breuder  reiterated  that  the  recent 
enactment  of  Act  44,  achieved  in  part  through 
the  substantial  efforts  of  the  College,  was  the 
most  infiuenlical  single  factor  in  minimizing 
costs  to  sponsor  districts  and  students. 

The  College  recently  credited  $295,000  back 
to  the  districts  for  the  current  fiscal  year  follow- 
ing enactment  of  that  legislation,  he  said.  He 
also  indicated  his  conviction  that  long-term 
financial  relief  to  sponsor  districts  is  on  the 
horizon.  College  officials  expect  recommenda- 
tions of  the  Governor's  Commission  on  Financ- 
ing Higher  Education  ~  scheduled  to  be  for- 
mulated in  October  of  this  year  -  will  result  in 
substantial  remedies  to  present  funding  pro- 
blems. 

Other  legislation  pending  the  General 
Assembly,  such  as  SBl  163  sponsored  by  Senator 
Robert  C.  Jubelirer,  of  Altoona,  would  greatly 
increase  the  state's  share  of  funding  of  com- 
munity colleges. 


"We  have  not  seen  more  promising 
developments  at  the  state  level  since  the  incep- 
tion of  community  colleges  in  Pennsylvania  20 
years  ago.  The  College  has  assigned  highest 
priority,  and  dedicated  unprecedented  energies, 
to  efforts  to  insure  those  state  initiatives  become 
law,"  said  Dr.  Breuder. 

The  proposed  budget  was  transmitted  to  the 
College'?  20-member  Executive  Council  on 
March  9,  was  to  have  been  presented  to  the 
Council  at  its  meeting  on  March  14,  and  will  be 
presented  on  March  29. 

The  College  Board  of  Trustees  has  given 
preliminary  approval  to  the  budget.  Final  action 
is  expected  on  the  proposed  budget  at  the 
board's  April  5  meeting.  After  board  action  on 
the  budget,  it  will  be  voted  on  by  each  of  the 
College's  sponsor  districts.  An  affirmative  vote 
by  14  of  the  20  sponsors  is  required  for  final  ap- 
proval. 

Enrollment  up 

from  page  2 

time  students  and  75  part-time  students.  The 
largest  group  of  students  (68)  is  not  seeking  a 
particular  degree. 

Tioga  County,  home  of  the  campus,  ac- 
counts for  93.5  percent  of  all  North  Campus 
students  and  Wellsboro  Area  School  District 
(seat  of  the  county)  sponsors  the  greatest  share 
of  North  Campus  students  (72). 

Last  spring,  Wellsboro  sponsored  42 
students;  this  Spring  their  total  sponsorship 
enrollment  is  114  students  ~  72  on  the  North 
Campus  and  42  on  central  campus.  The  mean 
age  of  the  North  Campus  student  is  30  years, 
compared  to  the  central  campus  mean  of  24 
years. 

Computer  Science  Club 
meets  next  Tuesday 

The  Computer  Science  Club's  next  meeting 
will  be  held  at  3:30  p.m.,  next  Tuesday  in  Room 
321,  Academic  Center  (ACC),  according  to  B. 
Drew  Robison,  club  public  relations  officer  and 
computer  science  student  from  Duncansville. 

Topics  to  be  discussed  are  a  picnic  for  club 
members,  to  be  held  at  the  end  of  the  semester, 
and  special  tours  for  club  members.

Computer  Science  Club,  faculty 

basketball  game 

to  be  played  Thursday  evening 

B.  Drew  Robison,  Computer  Science  Club 
public  relations  officer  and  computer  science  stu- 
dent from  Duncansville,  says  he  is  urging  club 
members  to  sign  up  for  a  basketball  game 
against  the  faculty  to  be  held  on  Thursday  even- 
ing in  the  Bardo  Gym. 

"There  will  be  a  practice  later  today  for 
members  who  sign  up,"  said  Robison. 

Members  who  wish  to  sign  up  or  anyone 
with  further  questions  about  the  game  may  con- 
tact David  A.  Lott,  instructor,  computer  science, 
in  Room  305,  Academic  Center,  or  on  College 
Ext.  228. 

Tennis  practice  scheduled  today 

The  tennis  team  will  have  its  first  practice 
today  from  3  to  5  p.m.  at  the  Memorial  Courts, 
weather  permitting,  according  to  Coach  James  S. 
Young,  carpentry  instructor. 

Students  interested  who  haven't  previously 
signed  up  may  go  to  the  courts  today  to  begin 
practices,  added  Young. 

The  first  match  is  scheduled  for  April  3.

Tuesday,  March  20,  1984     â€˘     SPOTLIGHT    â€˘ 

SGA  aids  Cancer  Society 
with  daffodil  sale 

The  Student  Government  Association  (SGA)  will  be  taking  orders  to  aid  the  American 
Cancer  Society  Daffodil  Days  Campaign,  according  to  Mrs.  JoAnn  R.  Fremiotti,  coordinator 
of  intramural  athletics  and  College  activities. 

This  is  a  state-wide  event  in  Pennsylvania  this  Thursday,  Friday,  and  Saturday.  The  daf- 
fodil is  the  flower  of  hope  for  cancer  patients,  said  Mrs.  Fremiotti. 

As  volunteers  for  the  American  Caner  Society,  students  will  be  taking  orders  for  daffodils 
until  tomorrow.   Price  of  the  daffodils  is  $3  for  10  daffodils,  she  said. 

Delivery  date  for  the  flowers  will  be  this  Thursday  and  Friday.  Money  will  be  collected  at 
the  time  of  delivery. 

Daffodils  can  be  ordered  from  any  member  of  the  SGA  or  by  calling  College  Ext.  269 
said  Mrs.  Fremiotti. 

The  money  raised  from  Daffodil  Days  goes  for  caner  research  and  patient  services. 


Women's  Week 
begins  Monday 

Dr.  Penelope  P.  Russianoff  will  be  the 
keynote  speaker  for  Women's  Week  which 
begins  next  Monday,  March  26  and  com- 
tinues  through  next  Friday,  March  30,  ac- 
cording to  Mrs.  JoAnn  R.  Fremiotti,  coor- 
dinator of  intramural  athletics  and  College 
activities. 

Films,  discussions,  and  lunches  have 
been  planned  throughout  the  week,  said 
Mrs.  Fremiotti.

Eight  PBL  members  to  attend 
State  Leadership  Conference 


Paul  W.  Goldfeder,  assistant  professor  of 
business  administration;  Miss  Valerie  J. 
Haydock,  coordinator  of  the  Individualized 
Learning  Center  for  Typewriting  and  Word  Pro- 
cessing, and  eight  Phi  Beta  Lambda  (PBL) 
members  will  attend  the  13th  Annual  State 
Leadership  Conference  on  April  6,  7,  and  8. 

The  conference  will  be  held  at  the  Marriott 
Hotel  in  Harrisburg,  according  to  Goldfeder. 

Pennsylvania  chapters  of  Phi  Beta  Lambda 
will  compete  in  several  business-related  testing 
categories  including  accounting,  typing,  business 
communications,  business  law,  data  procesing, 
information  management,  economics,  word  pro- 
cessing, and  public  relations. 

According  to  David  A.  Haas,  PBL  club 
president  and  computer  science  student  from 
Williamsport,  winning  students  receive  trophies

through  9.

will  attend  are  Elizabeth  A.  Zerby,  secretarial 
science.  Avis;  Sheryl  L.  Wilkins,  secretarial

day in  Harrisburg.  The  main  thrust  of  the 
meeting,  said  Goldfeder,  was  to  complete  plans 
for  the  conference.

Tuesday,  March  20,  1984     â€˘     SPOTLIGHT     â€˘ 

Dr.  Breuder  presents  update 
report  to  Chamber  of  Commerce 

By  Thomas  H.  Long 
Of  The  SPOTLIGHT  Staff 

At  a  breakfast  meeting  last  Tuesday  at  The  Hillside  Restaurant,  Dr.  Robert  L.  Breuder, 
College  president,  presented  a  College  update  to  the  Williamsport-Lycoming  Chamber  of  Com- 
merce. 

Despite  stormy  weather,  the  meeting  was  well  attended.  The  approximately  75  people  in- 
cluded members  of  the  Chamber  of  Commerce,  College  faculty  and  staff,  and  members  of  the 
press. 

Dr.  Breuder  spoke  about  the  need  for  updating  at  the  College  due  to  technological  ad- 
vancements in  all  facets  of  the  world  of  work.  One  step  presently  being  undertaken  at  the  Col- 
lege is  the  new  Advanced  Technology  and  Health  Services  Center  slated  to  be  built  beginning  in 
Spring  1985. 

Figures  presented 

Included  in  the  new  building  will  be  computer  technology,  dental  hygiene,  electronics, 
graphic  arts,  nursing  (LPN),  radiologic  technology,  respiratory  therapy,  surgical  technology, 
and  word  processing.   All  these  are  to  be  relocated  or  expanded  in  the  new  building. 

Curriculums  proposed  for  the  new  Advanced  Technology  Center  include  automated 
manufacturing  processing,  avionics,  biomedical  equipment  repair,  electronic  specialities,  laser 
technology,  nuclear  medicine,  nursing  (RN),  robotics,  and  speciality  labs  which  will  include 
electro-mechanics  and  hydraulics. 

Dr.  Breuder  presented  preliminary  figures  of  the  projected  cost  relating  to  the  project. 
The  complete  facility  will  cost  $14,875,000  and  will  cover  125,000  square  feet.  He  added  that 
completion  of  the  buildings  should  be  in  the  summer  1987. 
'...into  the  future...' 

Besides  the  latest  building  addition,  updating  in  the  other  labs  is  also  being  done.  With 
the  new  computer  assisted  design  and  computer  assisted  manufacturing  (CAD/CAM),  pro- 
grams such  as  machine  tool  technology  and  word  processing  will  be  "launched  into  the  age  of 
future  technology,"  added  Dr.  Breuder.  The  system  will  cost  the  College  an  estimated 
$600,000. 

Other  labs  will  be  added  or  expanded  to  accomodate  technological  advancements,  accor- 
ding to  Dr.  Breuder.  The  cost  to  equip  these  labs  will  be  approximately  $1  million,  but  all  the 
funds  for  this  undertaking  will  come  from  the  federal  government  or  special  programs.  Dr. 
Breuder  pointed  out  thai  none  of  this  money  will  come  whhin  the  College  or  its  sponsoring 
districts,  thus  easing  any  budget  strain. 

In  concluding  the  report.  Dr.  Breuder  asked  for  input  from  the  Chamber  of  Commerce  on 
any  additions  or  deletions  members  feel  appropriate  regarding  the  College  updating  and 
upgrading  of  curriculums.

Tuesday,  March  20,  1984 


Counselors  attend 
annual  session 

Several  members  of  the  staff  of  Counseling, 
Career  Development,  and  Placement  Office  at- 
tended the  Annual  Conference  of  the  Penn- 
sylvania College  Personnel  Association  held  Feb. 
26  to  28  in  Grantville,  Pa.,  according  to 
Lawrence  W.  Emery  Jr.,  director  of  counseling, 
career  development,  and  placement  at  the  Col- 
lege. 

Emery,  who  was  the  conference  chairper- 
son, is  president-elect  of  the  Pennsylvania  Col- 
lege Personnel  Association  and  will  assume  th 
presidency  on  June  1,  1984. 

G.  Robert  Converse,  coordinator  of  the 
career  options  for  displaced  homemakers  pro- 
gram presented  a  workshop  entitled  "Videotape 
Production  for  Recruitment,  Career  Education, 
and  Counseling",  which  featured  the  recently- 
produced  videotapes  of  the  Williamsport  Area 
Community  College. 

Also  attending  were  Thomas  Shoff  and 
Thomas  McNally,  counselors  at  the  College. 

The  Pennsylvania  College  Personnel 
Association  is  a  division  of  the  American  College 
Personnel  Association  and  has  over  400 
members  representing  student  affairs  at  all  levels 
of  postsecondary  education  in  Pennsylvania. 


Plans  for  TOP  program 
announced  by  president 

Speaking  to  the  Williamsport-Lycoming 
Chamber  of  Commerce,  Dr.  Robert  L.  Breuder, 
College  president,  announced  plans  for  a  new  in- 
dustry training  program.  The  program  is  to  be 
called  Training  for  Occupations  with  Promise 
(TOP). 

The  purpose  of  the  program  is  to  fund 
training  programs  to  prepare  the  unemployed  or 
underemployed  adults  for  occupations  experienc- 
ing growth  or  to  provide  skills  to  improve  perfor- 
mance in  current  jobs,  according  to  Dr.  Breuder. 

At  the  meeting,  last  Tuesday  morning,  Dr. 
Breuder  distributed  an  outline  of  the  new  pro- 
gram which  included  its  purpose,  cost,  available 
funding,  and  eligibility. 

A  minimum  of  $450,000  is  available  for 
program  funding.  However,  this  is  on  a  first 
come,  first  served  basis,  added  Dr.  Breuder. 

In  addition  to  funding,  employers  who  are 
planning  to  improve  employees'  skills  are  en- 
couraged to  participate  in  meeting  15  percent  of 
the  total  training  cost.  They  are  not  required, 
however,  said  Dr.  Breuder. 

This  program,  which  will  be  ftmded  from 
July  1,  1984  through  June  30,  1985,  is  open  to 
any  adult  needing  training  to  obtain  or  even 
maintain  skilled  labor,  said  Dr.  Breuder.

Tuesday,  March  20,  1984 


First-year  broadcasting 
students  assume  duties 

The  first-year  students  of  the  College's 
student-operated  radio  station  (WWAS)  have 
assumed  most  of  the  duties  traditionally  held  by 
second-year  students,  according  to  Bill  L. 
Phoenix,  broadcasting  student  from  Lock  Haven 
and  promotion  director. 

The  early  turnover  is  done  to  develop  the 
first-year  students  in  their  respective  manage- 
ment positions  for  the  upcoming  semester, 
Phoenix  said. 

The  management  team  is  primarily  second- 
year  students  with  the  exception  of  the  news 
department,  where  Wesley  H.  Latchford,  first- 
year  student  from  Altoona,  is  in  charge.  He  has 
made  a  vast  improvement  in  the  department, 
said  Phoenix. 

The  second-year  students  of  the  manage- 
ment team  must  still  account  for  their  perfor- 
mance of  their  successors,  according  to  Phoenix. 

A  list  of  the  air  time  schedule  follows: 

From  8  a.m.  to  10  a.m.,  classical  music; 
from  10  a.m.  to  12  p.m.,  jazz;  12  p.m.  to  6 
p.m.,  top  40,  and  from  6  p.m.  to  1  a.m.,  rock 
and  roll. 

The  news  will  be  covered  during  60  second 
intervals,  at  a  quarter-after-the-hour,  between  1 
and  5  p.m.,  according  to  Phoenix. 

Table  tennis  cancelled 

The  table  tennis  tournament  was  cancelled 
due  to  lack  of  interest,  according  to  Mrs.  JoAnn 
R.  Fremiotti,  coordinator  of  intramural  athletics 
and  College  activities. 

Students  wanting  to  play  table  tennis  during 
open  gym  hours  may  request  equipment  in  ad- 
vance by  contacting  Mrs.  Fremiotti,  Room  106, 
Bardo  Gym. 

Dr.  Ian  Henderson 
to  perform 

Dr.  Ian  Henderson,  professor 
emeritus  of  music  at  the  State  University 
College,  Brockport,  N.Y.,  will  be  at  the 
College  at  8  p.m.  this  Thursday  in  the 
Academic  Center  Auditorium  to  perform 
on  his  double  harpsichord  and  clavichord, 
according  to  Mrs.  JoAnn  R,  Fremiotti, 
coordinator  of  intramural  athletics  and 
College  activities.

State  inspection, 
vehicle  tune-up  offered 

In  the  Automotive  Technology  Division, 
state  inspection  and  vehicle  tune-up  services  are 
being  offered,  according  to  Wilham  H.  Debolt, 
director  of  transportation  technology. 

Vehicles  to  be  inspected  cannot  be 
"stickered",  but  student  labor  will  be  provided 
for  whatever  sections  of  the  vehicle  need  atten- 
tion, said  Rodman  H.  Perry,  instructor, 
automotive. 

The  vehicle  owner  must  provide  the  nec- 
essary parts,  and  a  $3  lab  fee  will  be  incurred,  he 
said.  Those  who  want  to  set  up  an  inspection 
appointment  may  call  Perry  at  College  Ext.  433 
after  9  a.m. 

Owners  of  vehicles  to  be  tuned-up  should 
bring  whatever  parts  will  be  needed,  according  to 
Lawrence  P.  Stabler,  auto  instructor.  There  is 
also  a  $3  fee  for  this  service,  he  said.  Appoint- 
ments may  be  made  by  calling  Stabler  on  College 
Ext.  432  after  9  a.m.

Williamsport  Area  Community  College 
'Opportunity  With  Excellence' 


2    â€˘    SPOTLIOHT  SPECIAL    â€˘    Open  House  Sunday,  March  25, 1984 


Welcome  to  Open  House  1984 
at  Your  Community  College 


I  refer  to  The  Williamsport  Area 
Community  College  as  "your"  Community 
College  to  emphasize  the  Importance  we  at  the 
College  place  on  responding  to  your  needs 
and  supporting  your  goals. 

During  Open  House  activities  today,  we 
will  show  you  just  how  we  are  able  to 
respond  to  your  needs  by  providing 
opportunities  for  you  to  create 
new  opportunities 

for  yourself.  By  providing  new  skills 
training,  upgrading  your  present  skills 
or  simply  increasing  your  knowledge 
of  the  world 

around  you.  The  Williamsport  Area  Community 
College  can  help  you  prepare  for 
a  successful  future, 

Our  theme  for  Open  House  1984  is 
"Opportunity  With  Excellence".  We're  certain 
you'll  find  this  theme  emphasized  throughout 
the  College  by  our  students  and  staff  in  our 
associate  degree  and  certificate  programs,  our 
secondary  vocational-technical  programs,  and 
our  non-credit  lifelong  education  programs. 

We're  very  proud  to  be  a  part  of  your 
Community  College.  And,  we  feel  sure  that 
when  you  leave  here  today,  you  too  will  be 
proud  to  be  a  part  of  The  Williamsport  Area

Opan  HouM  Sunday,  March  25, 1984     â€˘     SPOTirOHT  SPECIAL     â€˘ 

It's  easy  if  you  know  how: 

heavy  equipment  gets  'ride' 

in  unique  'rodeo' 

At  this  year's  Open  House,  a  unique  "rodeo"  is  tal<ing  place  at  the  Earth  Science 
Campus  at  Allenwood.  Being  staged  from  noon  until  4:30  p.m.  today,  the  event  takes 
place  in  the  parking  lot  area  at  that  campus. 
The  rodeo  is  being  sponsored  by  the  Service  and  Operation  of  Heavy  Construction 
Equipment  (S&O)  faculty,  the  S&O  Club,  and  the  S&O  Advisory  Committee. 
Approximately  30  to  35  high  school  students  are  participating  in  the  rodeo 
Represented  are  Centre  County  Vo-Tech.  Pleasant  Gap;  Wildwood  Career  Center, 
Hornell,  N.Y.;  Schuyler-Chemung-Tioga  County  B.O.C.E.S.,  Elmira.  N.Y..  and  the  Com- 
munity College 
Contests  include  forklifts,  rubber-tired  backhoes.  bulldozers,  and  track  backhoes.  The 
forkllft  contest  is  being  sponsored  and  judged  by  Forklifts  Inc.,  of  Williamsport.   In  this 
event,  students'  skills  will  be  taxed  when  they  drive  forklifts  through  an  obstacle  course 

to  achieve  points. 

The  bulldozer  event  is  a  material  handling  contest  in  which  the  students  push  a  rock 

around  the  perimeter  of  a  circle  while  maintaining  control  of  the  vehicle.   The  event  will 

be  judged  on  the  basis  of  elapsed  time. 
In  the  rubber-tired  backhoe  event,  the  object  is  to  move  an  egg  from  a  box  of  sand, 
swing  the  machine  and  deposit  it  in  a  four-Inch  piece  of  pipe  without  dropping  or  break- 
ing the  egg's  shell.  The  hardest  part  of  the  contest  is  that  the  egg  must  be  picked  up  by 
a  tablespoon  attached  to  the  bucket  of  the  backhoe.  The  event  is  judged  on  the  basis  of 

time  also. 

The  track  backhoe  event  calls  for  the  contestants  to  swing  the  machine  in  a 

360-degree  circle  while  touching  objects  without  knocking  them  over.  This  will  also  be  a 

timed  event. 

Prizes  and  trophies  will  be  presented  at  a  catered  dinner  following  the  Open  House. 

Ivlembers  of  the  S&O  Advisory  Committee  will  serve  as  judges. 

Faculty  coordinators  include  Benjamin  H.  Eldred,  assistant  professor  of  S&O;  William 

L.  Stevens,  assistant  professor  of  S&O;  Scoti  B.  Appleman,  instructor  of  S&O;  Paul  J. 

Zell  Jr.,  instructor  of  S&O,  and  Dan  C.  Emick,  instructor  of  S&O. 


SPOTLIGHT  SPECIAL:  OPEN  HOUSE  SUNDAY  '84 
The  SPOTLIGHT  is  the  student  newspaper  of  the  Williamsport  Area  Community  Col- 
lege, Williamsport,  Pa.  1 7701 .  This  SPOTLIGHT  SPECIAL:  OPEN  HOUSE  SUNDAY  is  a  sup- 
plement to  the  newspaper's  regular  Ivlonday  morning  edition.  This  supplement  is  produced 
by  underclass  journalism  and  other  students  independent  of  supervision  by  upperclass 
editors  as  a  learning  experience. 

PRODUCTION  TEAI^  FOR  THE  SPOTLIGHT  SPECIAL 
Those  listed  have  participated  in  the  production  in  a  variety  of  ways  in  the  interest  of 
garnering  varied  experience:  Rodney  D.  Hill,  Kathryn  Ivl.  Gilbert,  Richard  E.  Kopp  Jr..  Gisela 
D.  Grassley,  Donna  1^.  Barnett,  James  K.  Morrissey,  and  Ivlark  S.  Schwanke.  Faculty  ad- 
viser: Anthony  N.  Cillo,  associate  professor  of  journalism. 

The  underclass  staff  gratefully  acknowledges  the  assistance  of  the  following  rr^embers 
of  the  upperclass  in  this  production:  Mary  L.  Pease,  Barbi  L.  Chilson,  Lori  M.  Lane,  and 
Thomas  H.  Long.

sciences,  in  individual  studies,  and  In  technology  studies,  are  being  presented.  These 

programs  generally  are  designed  for  students  wishing  to  transfer  to  four-year  colleges.

Secondary  Vocational  Programs 

presenting  various  displays 

and  projects  related  to  courses 

Open  House  visitors  have  the  opportunity 

to  explore  the  College's  Secondary  Vocational  Programs.   In 

masonry  and  carpentry,  visitors  will  tind  a  front  view  of  a  model  house  with  attached 

garage,  fireplaces  in  brick  and  stone,  a  wood  stove  installation,  a  cutaway  view  of  the 

fireplace,  an  outdoor  barbecue  and  masonary  arches.   All  of  these  displays  will  be  found 

in  Room  1 10  of  the  Building  Trades  Center. 

In  Room  1 30.  building  trades  projects  in  interior  trim,  in  drywall,-doors,  molding, 

ceilings  and  windows  (all  the  details  that  turn  a  shell  into  a  building)  will  be  on  display. 

Secondary  students  will  demonstrate  their  skills  in  machine,  architectural,  civil  and 

electronic  drafting,  as  well  as  in  technical  illustrations.   Students  will  also  demonstrate 

how  a  blueprint  machine  works.   All  of  the  secondary  drafting  displays  will  be  in  Room 

1 43  of  the  Adminstration  Building. 
Students  will  show  oft  their  machining  projects  in  the  machining  lab  of  the  Technical 
Trades  Center.   In  the  welding  shop,  next  door  in  the  Metal  Trades  Center,  welding 

projects  will  be  on  display. 
What's  it  like  to  be  a  student  in  the  secondary  auto    mechanics  program?  In  the 
auto  mechanics  shops  in  the  Technical  Trades  Center,  secondary  students  will 
demonstrate  brake  lathes,  alignment  equipment,  wheel  balancing  and  more. 
In  the  auto  body  repair  shop  in  Room  1 1 6  of  the  Automotive  Trades  Center,  secon- 
dary students  will  be  showing  works  in  progess  as  well  as  completed  projects.   Antique 
cars  will  also  be  displayed  throughout  the  Center. 
At  the  south  end  of  the  Administration  Building,  secondary  students,  who  will 
become  tomorrow's  electricians,  will  demonstrate  residential  and  Industrial  wiring, 
motors,  generators,  motor  control,  signs,  and  special  projects. 
At  the  Aviation  Center  in  f^ontoursville,  secondary  students  will  demonstrate  test 
equipment  like  ultrasound,  eddy  current,  magnaflux  and  zyglo.   A  display  of  aircraft  parts 
and  cutaway  displays  of  reciprocating  and  jet  aircraft  engines  will  also  be  available  for 

the  public  to  view. 

A  number  of  secondary  students  and  staff  will  also  be  available  at  the  Earth  Science 

Center  to  talk  about  their  programs  and  display  their  projects.   Horticulture  students  will 

give  a  classroom  demonstration  of  an  automatic  seeder.  They'll  also  have  displays  of 

potted  plants  and  silk  and  fresh  flower  arrangements.   Forestry  students  will  have 

displays  on  surveying  and  logs  and  lumber,  and  will  be  giving  demonstrations  at  the 

sawmill.   In  the  small  engine  shop,  visitors  will  find  engine  and  equipment  displays  that 

show  how  students  sharpen  their  skills. 

Health  assistant  program  students  will  provide  free  health  screening,  including  blood 

pressure,  pulse  rate,  height  and  weight  and  diabetic  screening, 

in  Room  405  of  the  Academic 

Center.

Hmmm...  just  like  TV? 

weather  maps  on  display 

in  electronics  technology 

area  in  Admin  Building 

A  live  reception  of  satellite  weather  maps 
direct  from  the  Geostationary  Weather  Satellite 
(on  a  cathode  ray  tube)  is  being  featured  in  the 
electronics  technology  lab  in  Room  1 1 4  of  the 
Administration  Building. 
The  entire  reception  system,  from  the 
parabolic  antenna  to  the  map  displays,  has  been 
built  by  students. 
In  the  microprocessor  lab,  student  "fun  pro- 
jects" in  electronic  music  and  games  are  being 
shown. 
In  this  area,  too,  a  student-built  robot  Is  be- 
ing used  to  demonstrate  learning  skills.

viding courtesy  coffee,  punch,  and  cookies  for  visitors  to  the  student  newspaper  office  In 

the  basement  of  the  Academic  Center.

Open  Hous*  Sunday,  March  25,  1984    â€˘    SPOTLIGHT  SPECIAL 


Nursing,  radiologic  technology 
dental  hygiene  programs  featured 
on  fourth  floor,  Academic  Center 

Information  about  the  College's  programs  in  praclical  nursing,  surgical  tectinology, 
radiologic  technology,  and  dental  tiygiene  is  available  on  the  fourth  floor  of  the  Academic 

Center. 

Practical  Nursing,  Surgical  Technology 

Displays  on  nutrition  and  cancer  are  in  Rooms  404  and  409. 

In  Room  401 ,  visitors  are  able  to  tour  the  surgical  technology  lab. 

Faculty  is  available  to  review  opportunities  in  both  programs. 

Radiologic  Technology 

In  Room  41 3,  information  -  including  information  about  x-rays  -  about  radiologic 

technology  is  available. 

Tours  of  the  RT  lab  and  of  the  darkrooms  as  well  as  equipment  demonstrations  and 

an  informational  videotape  are  being  offered  regularly  on  the  hour. 

Basics  of  radiology  are  being  reviewed  in  discussions  and  mini-lectures. 

Dental  Hygiene 

Plaque  checks  and  oral  cancer  examinations  are  being  offered  continuously  during 

Open  House  in  the  Dental  Hygiene  Clinic  on  the  fourth  floor  of  the  Academic  Center. 


Drafting,  tool  design 
students  showing  worl( 

In  the  Administration  Building,  engineering 
drafting  students  will  be  working  throughout 
Open  House  hours. 
Visitors  may  see  how  draftsmen  and 
women  develop  the  drawings  and  plans  used  for 
manufacturing  and  engineering  projects. 
Engineering  and  industrial  drafting  projects 
and  displays  relate  to  basic  drafting,  power 
transmissions,  casting  detailing,  working  draw- 
ings, assembles,  mechanisms,  weldments,  pip- 
ing layouts,  civil  drafting,  and  architectural  draf- 
ting. 
Other  displays  include  fixture  design,  quality 
control  set-ups,  stamping  dies,  and  numerical 
control  programming.

Cadillac:  Automotive  Trades  Center

Center,  for  any  persons  interested  in  earning  a

classes.

available  in  Academic  Center

Open  House  visitors  will  be  able  to  see  a  display 
of  accounting  materials  and  learn  about  the  ac- 
counting curriculum  offered  at  the  College. 


Admissions  Office,  other 

support  service  offices 

open  to  give  answers 

How  do  I  go  about  enrolling? 

How  about  financial  aid? 

What  books  are  available? 

These  and  other  questions  can  be 
answered  by  a  visit  to  one  of  the  College's  sup- 
port services  offices.   Open  today  are  the  Col 
lege  Bookstore,  the  Ivledia  Center,  the  Admis- 
sions Office,  the  Financial  Aid  Office, 
Developmental  Studies, 
and  Student  Health  Services 
Staff  is  available  in  each  location  to  answer 
specific  questions  about  admission  re- 
quirements, about  help  In  financing  an  educa- 
tion, about  counseling,  and  so  on. 
The  Admissions  Office  is  on  the  first  floor  of 
the  Academic  Center.  The  Financial  Aid  Office 
is  located  on  the  second  floor  of  the  Academic 
Center. 
The  Bookstore.  Development  Studies,  and 
Ivledia  Center  are  located  In  the  Learning 
Resources  Center. 
Student  Health  Services  is  located  in  the 
gymnasium.

18     â€˘     SPOTLIGHT  SPECIAL     â€˘     OpÂ«n  HouM  Sunday,  March  25,  1 984 

Bus  available  to  take  visitors 
to  Earth  Science  Campus  to  see 
heavy  equipment  rodeo,  forestry 

and  variety  of  other  exhibits 

Open  House  visitors  interested  in  learning  more  about  nature,  flowers,  trees,  dairy 
production,  small  engines  -  or  in  seeing  a  "heavy  equipment  rodeo"  may  take  advantage 
of  free  bus  trips  from  the  city  campus  to  the  College's  Earth  Science  Campus  at  Allen- 
wood. 
The  bus  will  be  leaving  today  from  the  Learning  Resources  Center  -  on  West  Third 
Street  -  every  half  hour  starting  at  1 2:30  p.m.  and  continuing  until  3:30  p.m. 
Return  trips  are  scheduled  at  1 ,  2,  3,  and  4:30  p.m.,  according  to  Glenn  fl.  Spoerke, 
acting  director  of  the  Earth  Science  Division. 

Forestry  Technology 

The  forestry  technology  students  will  hold  a  log  skidder  contest  among  themselves. 

They  will  maneuver  the  log  skidder  through  a  pre-set  course  and  will  be  timed.   A  log 

skidder  is  used  to  remove  a  fallen  tree  from  the  woods. 

Surveying  Equipment 

A  display  on  surveying  equipment  used  by  students  is  scheduled  to  be  set  up  in 
Room  11 4  at  the  Earth  Science  Campus  and  information  will  be  provided  by  the  College 

Information  Office. 


Wood  Products  and  The  Sawmill 

Wood  products  technology  students  are  giving  tours  of  the  sawmill  which  is  located 
about  200  yards  from  the  central  building  and  give  demonstrations  showing  how  logs 

become  lumber. 
A  display  of  common  wood  products  available  in  the  marketplace  today  are  set  up 

in  Room  1 1 3. 

Dairy  Product  Taata  Test 

Agribusiness  and  dairy  herd  management  students  are  holding  a  dairy  product  taste 
test.   They  are  using  real  and  imitation  dairy  products. 

Computers 

Demonstrations  are  being  given  showing  how  the  computer  is  used  to  control  feeding  on 

the  farm. 

Farm  Equipment  Display 

Farm  equipment  and  animal  health  equipment  is  on  display.  An  actual  demonstration 

using  a  live  calf  shows  how  the  health  equipment  works.

About  Qardaning 

A  spring  gardening  and  seed  germination  slide  show  is  being  sliown  througfiout 
Open  House  liours.   Demonstrations  of  an  automatic  seeder  and  fiorai  design  tectiniques 

are  being  presented. 

Small  Engines  and  Equipment 

Secondary  vocational  technology  students  are  presenting  displays  of  small  engines 

and  equipment.  Visitors  are  being  invited  to  "look  around"  the  small  engine  shop  to  see 

what  high  school  students  use  in  their  learning  activities  during  the  18  weeks  they  spend 

on  the  College  campus. 

Flowers:  fresh,  silk,  and  dried 

Horticulture  students  are  presenting  a  display  of  silk,  dried,  and  fresh  flowers  in 
Room  1 1 5,  Earth  Science.   There  are  tours  of  the  greenhouse  available  all  day  long.   In- 
structors and  other  College  employees  are  on  hand  to  answer  questions. 

Heavy  Equipment  Rodeo 

Service  and  operation  of  heavy  construction  equipment  students  are  displaying 

hydraulic  hook-ups  and  pressure  gauges.  The  students  are  demonstrating  them  on  their 

equipment  to  show  how  the  so-called  "gadgets"  work. 

A  highlight  of  the  day  at  the  Earth  Science  Campus  is  the  "Heavy  Equipment 

Rodeo"  which  begins  at  noon. 

Got  a  chill? 

Solar  hot  water  heating 

exhibit  may  be  an  answer 

In  the  plumbing  and  heating  shops  in  the 

vicinity  of  Room  1 37  of  the  Building  Trades 

Center,  students  are  demonstrating  combustion 

testing  and  other  related  skills. 

In  the  same  section  are  displays  of  solar  hot 

water  heating,  wood  burning  heating,  and  other 

systems. 

Computer  science  faculty 

demonstrate 

microcomputers 

In  Room  321  of  the  Academic  Center, 
home  computer  applications,  electronic  spread- 
sheet analysis,  and  word  processing  is  being 
demonstrated  on  different  models  of  microcom- 
puters. 
Faculty  members  are  on  hand  to  explain  the 
relationship  of  these  activities  pertaining  to  the 
computer  science  curriculum.

Need  income  tax  help? 
Service  available  In  LRC 

The  Business  and  Computer  Science  Divi- 
sion is  offering  a  Voluntary  Income  Tax 
1  Assistance  (VITA)  Program  In  the  Tutorial  Center 
located  in  the  Learning  Resources  Center  (LRC). 
Fourth  semester  accounting  students  are 
assisting  anyone  who  would  like  help  with  their 
income  tax  returns. 
The  tax  service  is  free  and  will  be  continued 
until  Thursday,  April  12. 
Service  hours  are  3:30  p.m.  to  5:30  p.m.  on 
Mondays  and  Wednesdays  and  3:30  p.m.  to 
7:30  p.m.  on  Tuesdays  and  Thursdays. 


Financial  planning 
subject  of  video,  comment 

A  videotape  presentation  dealing  with  finan- 
cial planning  is  being  given  in  Room  319  of  the 
Academic  Center  during  Open  House  today. 
Ray  F,  Tyler,  associate  professor  of 
business  administration,  will  be  on  hand  to  ex- 
plain the  College's  business  management  cur- 
riculum. 


Visitors  may  'compute' 
in  Computer  Science  Lab 

The  Computer  Science  Laboratory,  in  Room 
314,  Academic  Center,  is  open  to  visitors  in- 
terested in  the  College's  computer  science  cur- 
riculum. 
Demonstrations,  explanations,  and  "hands 
on"  experiences  with  computers  are  being  of- 
fered. 
Computer  science  faculty  members  and 
students  are  available  to  answer  questions  about 
the  computer  science  program. 

Bulletin  boards  reflect 
secretarial  science  students  worl( 

Bulletin  boards  on  the  third  floor  of  the

secretarial  science  students. 

Their  work  on  the  boards  is  part  of  an 

organization  and  display  practice  related  to  their 

curriculum. 


Continental  breakfast 

and  dinner  offered 

in  cafeteria 

A  continental  breakfast  and  a  dinner  are  be- 
ing served  today  in  the  Canteen  Corp.-operated 
cafeteria  in  the  Academic  Center. 
The  breakfast  is  being  served  from  1 0  a.m. 
to  11  a.m. 
Dinner  is  being  served  from  1 1  a.m.  to  2:30 
p.m. 
The  dinner  menu  includes  fruit  cup.  roast  torn 
turkey,  home-made  stuffing,  creamy  mashed 
potatoes,  giblet  gravy,  buttered  cornniblets, 
cranberry  sauce,  hot  roll  and  butter,  and  home- 
made cake. 
The  cost  of  the  dinner  is  $3.75. 
Also  being  served,  a  la  carte,  are  milk, 
juices,  soft  drinks,  coffee,  tea,  and  home-made 
cherry  glazed  custards. 
Ms.  Lucy  F.  Twigg  is  chef.

Civil  engineering 
projects  on  display 


Open  House  visitors  today  may  see 
demonstrations  on  equipment  and  receive  infor- 
mation about  how  civil  engineers  design 
highways,  bridges,  dams,  and  other  projects  In

'Swinging  into  Spring' 

fashion  show  to  be  staged 

at  2:30  in  Auditorium 


"Swinging  into  Spring"  is  the  theme  of  a 
fashion  show  to  be  presented  by  fashion  mer- 
chandising students  in  the  retail  management 
program  at  2:30  p.m.  today  in  the  Academic 
Center  Auditorium. 
The  show's  purpose  is  to  demonstrate  the 
proper  attire  for  success  in  the  business  world. 
Faculty  and  staff  members  will  model  casual 
sportswear,  business  and  officewear,  and  formal 
wear. 
The  show  is  part  of  the  students'  classwork. 
Outfits  for  the  show  are  provided  by  local  retail 
establishments.

with  welding: 

students  showing  how 

In  the  welding  shop  in  the  Metal  Trades 
Center,  students  are  demonstrating  welding  pro- 
cesses. 
Displays  on  welding  equipment  and 
materials  will  be  set  up. 
A  slide  program  on  welding  will  be  shown 
in  Room  1 07  of  the  Is/letal  Trades  Center, 


Graphic  arts  tours 
conducted  by  students 

The  Gamma  Epsilon  Tau  (graphic  arts 

fraternity)  members  will  host  Open  House  for  the 

graphic  arts  section  In  the  Administration 

Building,  according  to  Charles  E.  Earle,  graphic 

arts  student  from  Troy  and  club  member. 

The  club  members  will  conduct  tours  of  the 

department  and  give  demonstrations  of  the 

typesetting,  press,  and  camera  areas. 

In  addition  to  the  tours,  various  samples  of 

work  from  the  graphic  arts  courses  will  be 

distributed,  Earle  said. 

Faculty  members  will  also  be  present  to 

answer  questions.

Monday.  April  9.  1984 


Survey  shows  75  percent 

of  full-time  students 
'in  SPOTLIGHT  habit' 


Preliminary  compiialion  of  responses  to  a 
survey  being  conducled  in  conjunction  with 
dasswork  indicate'  75  percent  of  full-time 
students  at  the  College  are  "in  the  SPOTLIGHT 
habit",  according  to  Anthony  N.  Cillo,  adviser 
to  the  student  newspaper. 

The  survey  was  conducted  as  a  project  by 
students  enrolled  in  Principles  of  Advertising 
classes  in  order  to  simulate  a  market  study. 

Part  of  the  study  focuses  on  information 
about  the  people  in  the  simulated  market  and 
another  part  focuses  on  media  available  in  the 
simulated  market,  Cillo  said.  The  full-lime  stu- 
dent population  at  the  College  was  used  to  create 
the  simulated  market,  he  added. 

Cillo,  who  is  also  associate  professor  of 


Journalism,  said  the  survey  was  based  on  prin- 
ciples of  random  sampling.  Information 
gathered  will  be  used  by  students  enrolled  in  the 
classes  for  decision-making  about  advertising 
and  promotion  for  a  simulated  client. 

Among  the  questions  routinely  asked  about 
medfa  available  were  ones  about  the 
SPOTLIGHT.  Answers  showed  that  the  largest 
majority  of  students  pick  up  the  student 
newspaper  on  a  "regular"  basis  every  week  and 
the  next  largest  indicated  that  students  pick  up 
the  newspaper  "frequently". 

According  to  the  survey  responses,  another 
important  means  by  which  students  get  College 
information  is  "bulletin  boards".  Sixteen  per- 
cent of  the  respondents  said  bulletin  boards  are 
their  main  source  of  information. 


ThaVs  friendship!  Theater  group 
tells  it  like  Shakespeare  told  it 

The  Bloomsburg  Theatre  Ensemble  presented  "Shakespeare  and  Friends"  in  the 
Academic  Center  Auditorium  last  Tuesday. 

The  selection  of  five  scenes  from  the  plays  of  William  Shakespeare  dealing  with  friendship 
gave  a  close  look  at  the  emphasis  placed  on  friendship  in  his  works. 

The  troupe  of  four,  out  of  a  total  company  of  over  70,  which  appeared  at  the  College 
prefaced  each  scene  with  a  short  explanation  of  the  interactions  between  individuals  as  those  in- 
teractions specifically  revolved  around  friendship. 

Actors,  Martin  Schell  and  Charles  Matheny  and  actresses  Julie  Flood  and  Martha  Kemper 
opened  their  selections  with  a  scene  from  As  You  like  ll.  In  the  scfne,  two  female  cousins 
decide  to  run  away  and  live  in  the  woods  after  they  are  jilted. 

Their  performance  closed  with  a  scene  from  A  Midsummer  Night's  Dream  in  which 
the  couples  run  to  the  woods  and  stumble  into  love  spells  with  each  ofher.  The  shuffle  of  affec- 
tion was  true  comedy. 

Also  included  was  a  sonnet,  recited  by  Martha  Kemper,  simply  entitled  "Sonnets  for 
Friends". 

The  Bloomsburg  Theatre  Ensemble,  in  existence  for  seven  years,  puts  on  full-scale  produc- 
tions for  six  months  of  the  year.  For  the  remaining  months,  troupes  travel  to  various  college 
campuses  to  perform. 


Distinguished  Teaching 
Awards  committee 
reviewing  18  nominations 

The  selection  board  for  the  Distinguished 
Teaching  Award  has  been  holding  meetings 
recently  to  determine  this  year's  recipient,  said 
Dr.  Robert  G.  Bowers,  executive  assistant  for  in- 
ternal affairs.  A  total  of  18  teachers  from  the 
College  have  been  nominated. 

The  board  met  on  Thursday,  March  22  and 
again  last  Thursday,  at  which  time  they  applied 
the  nominations  to  the  award  criteria  outlined  in 
the  Distinguished  Teaching  Awards  booklet,  said 
Dr.  Bowers. 

Members  of  the  committe  consist  of  Dr. 
Bowers;  Dr.  Russell  C.  Mauch,  dean  of 
academic  affairs;  Ruth  A.  Fischer,  student 
government  representative,  and  three  immediate 
past  recipients  of  the  award. 

The  past  award  winners  are  Dr.  Peter  B. 
Dumanis,  professor  of  English,  1983,  and  Mrs. 
Veronica  M.  Muzic,  professor  of  English,  1982. 

Since  the  award  has  only  been  in  existence 
for  two  years,  and  so  there  are  only  two  previous 
winners,  a  third  judge  had  to  be  selected.  Dr. 
Bowers  said  Victor  A.  Michael  Sr.,  associate 
professor  of  electronics  was  selected.  Michael  is 
i  1982  recipient  of  an  Excellance  in  Teaching 
Award. 

Dr.  Bower  said  the  committee  should  be 
able  to  make  a  recommendation  by  April  20.  It 
will  present  the  choice  for  the  award  to  Dr. 
Robert  L.  Breuder,  College  president,  who  will 
make  the  final  decision  on  the  recipient,  added 
Dr.  Bowers. 

This  year's  recipient  will  be  announced  by 
Dr.  Breuder  at  the  commencement  on  May  12.

development,  and  placement. 

The  meeting  will  be  held  in  the  Presi- 
dent's Conference  Room  in  the  Ad- 
ministration Building.

Journalism  graduate 
named  ad  manager 
for  publication 

Yvonne  M.  Swartz,  a  1982  graduate  of  the 
journalism  program  at  the  College,  has  been  ap- 
pointed advertising  manager  for  The  Solar  Pro- 
fessional, a  publication  of  the  Northeast  Solar 
Energy  Industries  Association,  Stamford,  Conn. 

According  to  Anthony  N.  Cillo,  associate 
professor  of  journalism,  a  news  item  in  the 
publication  about  the  appointment,  states: 

"Miss  Swartz's  college  education  in  jour- 
nalism and  previous  professional  experience  with 
a  daily  newspaper  qualify  her  for  these  duties 
and  make  her  an  excellent  in-house  resource  for 
firms  not  using  ad  agencies." 

As  advertising  manager,  Miss  Swartz  will 
handle  the  publication's  advertising  accounts 
and  supervise  copy  preparation. 

While  attending  the  College,  Miss  Swartz 
was  a  member  of  The  SPOTLIGHT  staff  and 
served  one  term  as  managing  editor. 

It's  National  Library  Week; 
fines  to  be  'forgiven' 

This  week  is  National  Library  Week.  In 
observation  of  this  event,  the  Library  is  holding 
"forgiveness  week". 

During  this  time  period,  overdue  books  may 
be  returned  with  no  penalty  assessed,  according 
to  Mrs.  Kate  D.  Hickey,  College  librarian. 
Books  for  which  fines  have  already  been  assess- 
ed, however,  are  not  included,  she  added. 

In  observance  of  the  occasion,  too,  the 
Library  has  set  up  three  special  displays  featur- 
ing women's  studies,  featuring  anthropologist 
Margaret  Mead,  and  featuring  George 
Washington. 


Library  receives 
new  furniture 

The  College  Library  has  received 
new  furniture.  It  is  being  used  in  the 
study  area  on  the  second  floor,  accor- 
ding to  Mrs.  Kate  D.  Hickey,  College 
librarian.

Monday,  April  9,  1984     â€˘     SPOTLIGHT     â€˘ 

Disaster  movie  blends 
realism  and  courage 

Television  review  by  Anne  T.  Moratelli 

After  viewing  television's  Flight  1190:  Disaster  on  the  Potomac  last  Sunday  evening,  I 
came  to  the  conclusion  that  it  was  not  a  typical  disaster  movie- 
It  was  based  on  a  plane  crasti  whicti  occurred  on  Jan.  1 3. 1 982  on  a  737  commercial  jet 
enroute  to  Tampa,  Florida.  The  plane  crashed  shortly  alter  takeoff  into  the  14tH  Street 
Bridge  in  Washington,  D.C.  and  dropped  into  the  icy  Potomac  River. 

The  film  was  very  realistic.  The  viewer  could  really  feel  sympathy  and  compassion  for 
the  victims  who  survived  the  crash  and  held  on  helplessly  waiting  lor  help  to  arrive 

The  audience  witnesses  people  turning  blue  from  frostbite  and  clinging  on  ice  chunks 
for  safety. 

The  movie  was  blended  with  news  footage  and  recreations. 

The  movie  in  actuality  consisted  of  two  subjects;  The  crash  and  the  heroism  of  a  young 
man  named  Lenny  Skutnik  who  cared  enough  to  dive  into  the  frozen  waters  to  rescue  a 
young  woman  (played  by  actress,  Dinah  (Vlanoff).  It  is  important  to  know  that  Skutnik  was  not 
involved  In  the  crash  and  all  of  the  passengers  on  the  flight  were  all  strangers  to  him. 

The  bravery  and  courage  Skutnik  displayed  showed  that  there  are  still  heroes  left  in  this 
world. 

After  sitting  back  and  reflecting  on  this  movie,  I  think  it  was  worth  watching  because  it 
wasn't  just  another  typical  disaster  movie  -  it  was  a  movie  of  courage  and  it  was  well- 
dramatized! 

Gal  way  adds  a  flavor 
of  nostalgia  to  his  tape 

Music  review  by  Sandra  R.  Taylor 

James  Galway  brings  nostalgia  to  bloom  in  his  tape.  James  Galway,  The  Pachetbel 
Canon  and  Others. 

Galway  is  a  flutist.  In  his  tape,  he  is  accompanied  by  The  Sydney  Symphony  Orchestra. 
His  music  ranged  from  /  Started  A  Jol<e  to  the  Jamaican  Rumba,  to  the  ever  famous  Waltz- 
ing Matilda.  This  tape  sets  a  mood  of  gayety.  The  Long  White  Cloud  and  Motley  on  the 
Shore  send  you  sailing  over  calm  waters  or  dancing  barefoot  on  a  meadow  of  clovers  in  the 
Spring. 

Galway  can  also  be  seen  on  public  cable  television  -  with  or  without  an  orchestra.  As  a 
soloist,  his  performance  is  outstanding.  In  an  age  of  rock-n-roll,  the  chances  of  a  flutist  sur- 
viving are  slim.  That  should  attest  Galway's  talent.  If  you  listened  to  his  tape,  you  would  at- 
test to  it  yourself.

Monday,  April  9,  1984     â€˘     SPOTLIGHT     â€˘     7 

Business  Symposium 
has  'low'  turnout 
due  to  bad  weather; 
300  students  attend 

"Despite  the  fact  that  only  12  of  the  ex- 
pected 30  schools  showed  up,  the  event  went 
well,"  said  Paul  W.  Goldfeder,  assistant  pro- 
fessor of  business  administration,  about  the  10th 
Annual  Business  Symposium  held  at  the  College 
on  Friday,  March  30. 

Goldfeder  said  the  weather  on  the  previous 
Thursday  was  a  major  factor  for  the  lower  tur- 
nout for  the  academic  competition, 

"Several  schools  in  outlying  districts  called 
me  and  cancelled  their  appearances,"  said 
Goldfeder.  "A  total  of  300  students  did  show 
up,  however." 

The  retail  merchandising  students' 
"Fashion  Pizzazz"  fashion  show  was  a 
"tremendous  success",  said  Goldfeder. 
Students  modelled  for  and  produced  the  show 
under  the  direction  of  Robert  L.  Lyons,  assistant 
professor  of  retail  management. 

Overall  grand  prizes  were  awarded  to 
Milton  Area  High  School  and  Montgomery  Area 
High  School.  "Williamsport  Area  High  School 
won  the  overall  grand  prize  in  the  AAAA  Divi- 
sion," Goldfeder  said. 

Dr.  Robert  L,  Breuder,  College  president, 
and  Mario  Caldera,  chairman  of  the  College 
Board  of  Trustees,  presented  trophies  to  winning 
schools  and  students  at  an  awards  ceremony  in 
the  Academic  Center  (ACC)  Auditorium, 

Goldfeder  noted  that  "Dr,  Breuder  and  Mr. 
Caldera  were  overwhelmed  by  the  enthisiastic 
response  and  attitude  of  the  students  at  the 
awards  presentation,"

Monday,  April  9,  1984     â€˘     SPOTLIGHT     â€˘     9 


Planning  commission  chairman 
reviews  goals,  procedures 


By  Robert  0.  Hawk 
Of  The  SPOTLIGHT  Staff 

The  chairman  of  the  Williamsport  Planning 
Commission,  Dr. Fred  L.  Grogan,  spoke  before 
Dr.  Daniel  J.  Doyle's  state  and  local  government 
class  as  part  of  ongoing  series  concerning  all 
aspects  of  the  functions  of  state  and  local 
government. 

Dr.  Grogan  discussed  the  goals  and  objec- 
tives of  the  city  planning  commission  as  well  as 
its  deficiences. 

In  opening  his  comments.  Dr.  Grogan  said 
the  commission  is  a  volunteer  body  and  its 
members  are  appointed  by  the  mayor  and 
members  of  the  City  Council. 

The  basic  responsibility  of  the  commission 
is  to  the  City  Council. 

Recommendations  made 

The  commission  is  consulted  on  topics  rang- 
ing from  installation  of  stop  lights  at 
troublesome  intersections  to  the  consideration  of 
the  best  types  of  right-of-ways  for  public 
transportation. 

Recommendations  are  made  to  the  City 
Council  in  three  primary  ways: 

First,  the  area  of  recommendations  which 
provides  zoning  exceptions  is  that  of  "special  ex- 
ceptions". An  example  of  this  is  the  American 
Rescue  Workers  request  to  open  a  men's  housing 
facility  in  an  area  zoned  as  residential. 

The  request  of  that  organization  was 
granted  and,  in  the  particular  case,  an  exception 
has  been  made. 

The  second  instance  is  that  of  "conditional 
uses".  In  this  case,  certain  functions  in  certain 
neighborhoods  can  be  permitted  only  on  the 
basis  of  an  increase  or  improvement  of  services. 
(For  example:  more  parking  and  road  and 
sidewalk  repair). 

The  third  kind  of  recommendation  comes 
under  the  category  of  "special  approval"  which 
calls  for  certain  areas  of  the  city  to  be  blocked 
off  for  special  functions  (the  cultural  or  theater 
district). 

Other  decisions 

Although  the  role  of  the  city  planning  com- 
mission can  be  overridden  by  the  City  Council,  it 
also  makes  decision  on  administrative  issues  in- 


volving the  city. 

In  comments  which  related  directly  to  the 
power  of  the  commission,  Dr.  Grogan  stated, 
"When  I  was  appointed  to  the  board,  the  com- 
mission had  no  teeth." 

On  the  subject  of  how  it  has  changed  since, 
he  said,  "We  wanted  to  make  a  living  document 
to  make  sure  that  we  set  in  play  some  suggestion 
that  would  force  City  Council  to  get  some  of 
them  done." 

Specific  instances  of  actions  the  commission 
was  involved  in  during  the  formulation  of  the 
new  comprehensive  city  plan  were  not  always 
positive  experiences,  he  said. 

Recently,  the  City  Council  did  some  "foot- 
dragging"  on  condemning  some  buildings  until  a 
boy  was  beaten  up  and  killed  in  one  of  them,  he 
said. 

This  is  not  the  rule,  however,  and  the  cir- 
cumstances of  the  changes  are  usually  much  less 
critical. 

Student  housing  noltd 

In  the  center  city  section,  for  example,  the 
problem  of  student  housing  was  raised  by 
landlords  offering  rooms  and  apartments.  The 
property  owners  expressed  their  very  low  esteem 
of  students  as  tenants  and  expressed  these  feel- 
ings before  City  Council. 

The  basic  concern  in  the  matter  was  that 
more  student  housing  should  be  provided  by  the 
College  or  the  city. 

Tighter  supervision  asked 

Dr.  Grogan's  account  of  his  answer  was, 
"As  the  state  prohibits  community  colleges  from 
having  dormitories,  the  College  itself  would  have 
to  arrange  with  a  private  developer  to  construct 
this  housing.  However,  this  was  found  not  to  be 
profitable  to  the  developers  and,  as  a  result,  the 
planning  commission  recommended  tighter  code 
supervision  in  existing  student  housing." 

In  closing  remarks,  Dr.  Grogan  said  the  job 
of  the  city  planning  commissioner  is  not  an  easy 
one.  If  citizens  want  something  done  badly 
enough  they  can  keep  calling  the  City  Council  of- 
fice, he  said. 

"You'd  be  surprised  what  you  can  get  done 
if  you  keep  after  them,"  he  said. 


Monday,  April  9,  1984 


Middle  States  Team  on  Campus; 
accreditation  review  process  begins 


By  Mary  L.  Peaae 
Of  The  SPOTLIGHT  Staff 

A  dinner  and  informal  meeting  at 
Williamsport's  Ross  Club  last  night  was  to  have 
kicked  off  the  Middle  States  Evaluation  Team's 
visit  to  the  College,  according  to  Rodney  G. 
Hurley,  dean  of  educational  research,  planning 
and  evaluation. 

Approximately  60  College  administrative 
representatives  were  to  have  been  present. 

During  the  team's  visit,  which  will  extend 
through  Wednesday  afternoon,  members  will  be 
meeting  with  Student  Government  Association 
(SGA)  representatives  and  stopping  students  ran- 
domly in  various  hallways  and  buildings  to  ques- 
tion them  about  curriculums,  instruction,  and 
the  College  in  general. 

Already  an  accredited  institution  since 
1970,  the  College  now  is  being  evaluated  for 
reaffirmation,  with  particular  emphasis  placed 
on  the  long  range  planning  process. 

The  team  consists  of  Chairman  Robert  L. 
Cell,  Cecil  County  Community  College,  North 
East,  Md.;  Dennis  S.  Bartow,  dean  of  technical 
and  career  education,  Prince  George  Community 
College,  Largo,  Md.;  Charles  H.  Bohlen,  dean, 
University  Community  and  Technical  College, 
University  of  Toledo,  Oh.;  Robert  R.  Gallo, 
associate  professor  of  science,  Cayuga  County 
Community  College,   Auburn,   N.Y.;   Myma 


Goldenberg,  chair,  general  education  committee, 
professor  of  English  and  philosophy,  Mon- 
tgomery, Rockville,  Md.;  Barbaraann  H. 
Owad,  director  of  plannmg  research  and 
records,  Adirondack  Community  College,  Glen 
Falls,  N.Y.;  Gwendolyn  W.  Sanders,  dean  of 
student  services,  Deleware  Technical  and  Com- 
munity College,  Wilmington/Stanton  Campus, 
Wilmington,  Del.;  Anthony  Scaccia,  college 
business  officer,  SUNY  Agricultural  and 
Technical  College,  Delhi,  N.Y.;  Eleanor  F. 
Seminara,  director,  library,  Niagara  County 
Community  College,  Sanborn,  N.Y.  and 
Thomas  N.  Wilfrid,  chairperson.  Technology 
Division,  Mercer  County  Community  College, 
Trenton,  N.J. 

Also  working  with  the  team  is  James 
Hobbs,  an  education  administration  associate 
with  the  Bureau  of  Education  Field  Services,  and 
the  Pennsylvania  Department  of  Education, 
Harrisburg. 

Hurley  stated,  "All  the  people  involved  in 
long  range  planning  realize  that  the  planning 
doesn't  stop  after  the  Middle  States  Team  leaves. 
Three  more  months  of  long  range  planning  work 
will  continue  after  the  visit  -  most  of  this  work 
focusing  on  the  assessment  report." 

Also,  an  "extensive  review  and  develop- 
ment" follows  the  visit,  and  the  long  range  plan- 
ning process  will  be  following  a  "very  busy  agen- 
da" through  April,  added  Hurley.

Tennis  team  plays  first  match 


Members  of  the  College  tennis  team  played 
their  first  match  of  the  season  on  Tuesday,  April 
3,  losing  in  competition  that  was  closer  than  the 
score  indicates,  according  to  Coach  James  S. 
Young,  carpentry  instructor. 

The  score  was  seven  to  two  in  that  match 
which  was  won  by  Delaware  County  Communil)( 
College.  That  college,  as  winners,  boosted  its 
record  to  three  wins  and  no  losses,  commented 
Young.

Civil  engineering  technicians 
announce  raffle  winners 

The  Civil  Engineering  Technicians  Club  has 
announced  the  winners  of  its  Feb.  23  raffle,  ac- 
cording to  Lamont  E.  Butters,  associate  pro- 
fessor of  civil  engineering  technology  and  club 
adviser. 

The  drawing  took  place  March  23  in  the 
lobby  of  the  Learning  Resources  Center  (LRC). 
The  names  were  drawn  by  Mrs.  Dorothy  Slat- 
tery,  receptionist. 

The  winners  of  the  drawing  were:  First 
prize,  $100,  John  Zeafla,  1410  St.  James  Place, 
Williamsport;  second  prize,  $50,  Thomas 
Dwyer,  100  Lynd  St.,  Biossburg,  and  third 
prize,  $25,  Wally  Sanders,  1412  State  St., 
Shamokin. 

The  money  raised  from  the  raffle  will  be  us- 
ed by  the  students  for  a  field  trip  on  April  10,  to 
the  Smithsonian  Institute  in  Washington,  D.C., 
said  Butters. 


PBL  members  set 
installation  banquet 
Wednesday  evening 

Phi  Beta  Lambda  (PBL)  members  will  hold 
an  installation  banquet  for  newly-elected  officers 
at  6  p.m.,  Wednesday,  at  the  Sheraton  Motor 
Inn,  Williamsport. 

According  to  David  A.  Haas,  PBL  presi- 
dent and  computer  science  student  from 
Williamsport,  scheduled  events  for  the  banquet 
are  a  social  hour  from  6  p.m.  to  7  p.m.,  a  dinner 
from  7  p.m.  to  8  p.m.,  and  an  installation 
ceremony  from  8  p.m.  to  8:30  p.m. 

Thomas  C.  Leitzel,  assistant  director  of  the 
Business  and  Computer  Science  Division,  will  be 
guest  speaker  at  the  banquet. 

"Dancing  and  swimming  will  be  part  of  the 
evening's  activities,"  said  Paul  W.  Goldfeder, 
assistant  professor  of  business  administration. 


16     â€˘     SPOTLIGHT     â€˘     Monday,  April  9,  1984 


'Driving  under  the  influence'  laws 
based  on  blood  alcohol  content 


li^omalion  provided  by  Mn.  Jonel  R.  Quenmil.  R.S.,  qfthtHeallh 
Senicfi  Centtr- 

All  30  states  have  laws  with  various 
penalties  against  driving  under  the  influence  of 
alcohol.  Most  states  base  these  laws  on  a  per- 
son's blood  alcohol  content  (BAC),  as  determin- 
ed by  a  breath  test,  or  on  a  direct  analysis  of 
blood. 

For  the  BAC  lest  .10  percent  is  evidence  of 
being  legally  intoxicated. 

A  12-ounce  serving  of  beer  containing  four 
percent  of  alcohol  will  register  .02  on  the  breath 
test.  For  harder  substances  (whiskey  or  vodka), 
a   one-ounce   serving   containing   43    percent 


alcohol  will  register  .02. 

These  figures  are  calculated  for  a  person 
weighing  160  pounds.  For  persons  weighing  less, 
the  BAC  would  be  proportionately  higher.  For 
persons  weighing  more,  it  would  be  propor- 
tionately lower. 

A  person's  BAC  drops  about  .02  percent 
per  hour  if  no  more  drinks  are  consumed.  Ef- 
fects wear  off  at  a  fairly  constant  rate. 

If  a  160  pound  person  drinks  five  beers,  or 
five  shots  of  whiskey,  in  one  hour  on  an  empty 
stomach,  his  BAC  would  equal  .10  percent.  It 
would  take  three  more  hours  to  reach  the  less- 
than-.05-percent  level  or  a  non-dangerous  level. 


At  Danville  Famt... 


Students  use  'brains' 
to  help  researchers 
strengthen  knowledge 

Students  at  (he  Danville  Farm  have  recently 
been  involved  in  a  real  "brainy"  project. 

According  to  Robert  A.  Patton,  instructor 
of  dairy  herd  management  at  the  farm,  students 
recently  participated  in  a  project  partly  involving 
the  forwarding  of  cow  brains  to  The  Eunice  Ken- 
nedy Shriver  Center  at  Massachusetts  General 
Hospital. 

Palton  said  students  forwarded  seven  fetal 
brains  obtained  from  Taylor  Packing  in  Wyalus- 
ing.  The  organs,  he  said,  are  intended  to  aid 
researchers  at  the  center  gain  a  deeper 
understanding  of  senility  and  mental  illness  in 
humans. 

The  brains,  he  explained,  were  wrapped  in 
garbage  bags,  packed  in  ice,  and  sent  to  the 
center  in  ice  chests  and  garbage  cans  by  Federal 
Express. 

Individuals  at  the  center  were  "delighted" 
al  receiving  the  organs,  remarked  Patton.  They 
were  "overjoyed"  he  said,  adding  that  more 
brains  will  be  forwarded. 

The  ongoing  program  is  in  cooperation  with 
Harvard  Medical  School,  Patton  said. 


Women's  Forum  meets  today 

Women's  Week/Women's  Forum  will  meet 
at  noon  today,  in  Room  103,  Academic  Center 
(ACC),  according  to  Mrs.  Virginia  M.  Tom- 
pkins, of  the  College  Information  Office. 

Next  week,  a  lunchtime  series  will  be  con- 
tinued by  the  Women's  Week  organizers  due  to 
the  closing  of  the  College  on  Thursday,  March 
29. 

"Math/Science  Phobia",  the  subject  of  the 
series,  will  begin  at  noon  next  Wednesday,  April 
18,  Room  105,  Academic  Center  (ACC). 

Beverage  will  be  provided,  and  interested 
persons  may  bring  a  brown  bag  lunch,  said  Mrs. 
Tompkins. 

Bluegrass  Festival 
this  Thursday  in  gym 

The  Seventh  Annual  Bluegrass  Festival  will 
be  held  at  7:30  p.m.,  this  Thursday  in  the  Bardo 
Gymnasium,  according  to  Mrs.  JoAnn  R. 
Fremioiti,  coordinator  of  intramural  athletics 
and  College  activities. 

Featured  groups  will  include  Leon  Morris 
and  Associates,  The  Tompkin  County 
Horseflies,  The  Bluegrass  Express,  and  The  Step 
Cadets  (previously  called  the  Lumberjacks). 

Students  with  validated  student  ID's,  facul- 
ty, and  staff  will  be  able  to  attend  for  free. 
There  will  be  a  $2  admission  fee  for  all  others, 
said  Mrs.  Fremiotti. 


Monday.  April  9,  1984     Â«     SPOTLIGHT 


Thursday  holiday  still  on'; 
some  classes  have  to  be  made  up, 
but  there's  special  way  to  do  it 

Next  Thursday,  April  19,  will  remain  an  official  College  holiday,  according  lo  a  deadline 
report  by  Dr.  Rnssell  C.  Maoch,  dean  of  academic  affairs. 

Some  classes,  however,  will  have  (o  be  "made  up". 

Dr.  Mauch  explained: 

Scheduled  classes  which  were  missed  on  Thursday,  March  29  -  the  date  of  the  worst 
winter  storm  in  this  area  in  a  decade  -  will  have  to  be  made  up.  This  is  because  the  College 
must  conform  to  state  requirements  as  to  number  of  hours  of  meeting  time  per  course. 

The  special  way  in  which  these  classes  are  lo  be  made  up:  Instructors  will  add  time  to 
each  of  the  sessions  of  the  course  which  remain  --  building  up  additional  time  to  equal  the 
amount  of  time  missed  on  that  stormy  Thursday  night. 

As  far  as  the  classes  which  were  cancelled  earlier  on  a  Tuesday  night  because  of  a  dif- 
ferent storm  are  concerned,  the  dean  said  a  check  of  the  academic  calendar  and  number  of 
hoars  required  does  not  make  it  necessary  to  make  up  the  Tuesday  night  classes. 

He  said  the  decision  about  the  holiday  and  the  make-up  was  made  after  careful  review 
of  the  academic  calendar  and  legal  requirements. 

In  short:  Students  who  missed  day  classes  on  March  29,  no  make-up;  students  who 
missed  evening  classes  on  March  29,  make-up  by  adding  time,  and  students  who  missed 
because  of  the  earlier  Tuesday  evening  storm,  no  make-up. 


Cafeteria-naming  contest  deadline  is  this  Wednesday 

The  deadline  is  i  p.m.  this  Wednesday  to  enter  the  contest  to  name  the  cafeteria  in  the 
new  Lifelong  Education  Center. 

The  contest  is  being  conducted  by  the  Student  Government  Association. 

Entry  forms  are  available  at  various  campus  locations  as  well  as  in  this  edition  of  The 
SPOTLIGHT.  Entries  may  be  dropped  off  at  various  spots. 

Entry  form  boxes  are  in  the  Intramural  Athletics  and  College  Activities  OfTice  in  the  Gym- 
nasium, in  Room  204  of  the  Administration  Building,  and  in  the  Student  Government  Associa- 
tion Office  which  is  Room  136  in  the  Academic  Center.

Monday,  April  9,  1984 


Dr.  Breuder  presents  1984-85 
College  budget  proposal 

By  Thomis  H.  Long,  Administrative  Affairs  Editor 

To  present  the  recommendation  for  the  1984-85  College  budget.  Dr.  Robert  L.  Breuder, 
College  president,  held  a  press  conference  last  Thursday  morning.  He  announced  that  the 
budget  he  is  recommending  will  total  $16.7  million.  This  represents  a  7.9  percent  increase  over 
last  year's  budget. 

Regarding  the  proposed  budget.  Dr.  Breuder  said,  "This  plan  represents  the  lowest  in- 
crease in  three  years  and  reflects  extreme  care  in  targeting  of  resources  with  exceptional  sen- 
sitivity to  sponsor  costs  without  sacrifice  of  excellence  or  opportunity." 

The  new  tuition  policy  was  also  discussed  at  the  meeting.  Dr.  Breuder  state  that  the  new 
policy  will  result  in  a  better  lower  tuition,  based  on  those  who  carry  approximately  15  credits  a 
semester. 

Dr.  Breuder  stated  that  due  to  House  Bill  177,  passed  last  Fall,  an  immediate  cost  reduc- 
tion of  $295,000  to  sponsoring  districts  and  a  proposed  1984-85  student  cost,  which  is  5.1  per- 
cent less  than  approved  in  1983-84,  will  result.  The  balance  is  to  be  paid  by  the  Com- 
monwealth of  Pennsylvania. 

A  total  operating  budget  for  the  College  falls  into  three  main  categories.  They  are 
salaries,  benefits,  and  supplies. 

Actual  budgetary  figures  include  $8,818,819  for  all  salaries  at  the  College.  This  represents 
a  $664,052  hike  over  last  year's  salary  budget,  or  5.155  percent  increase. 
Employee  benefits  show  a  $186,373  increase,  bringing  the  1984-85  proposal  to  $2,228,805. 
This  shows  a  1.5  percent  increase. 

The  budget  proposal  allows  $122,152  for  supplies  for  1984-85.  The  increase  is  $8,188 
over  last  year,  or  a  .064  increase. 

The  budget  for  other  expenses,  as  outlined  in  the  proposal,  shows  a  hike  if  $235,091,  thus, 
bringing  the  total  to  $2,011,637. 

The  North  Campus  in  Wellsboro  has  a  proposed  operating  budget  of  $261,166  for  the 
1984-85  school  year. 

The  final  proposed  operating  budget  for  the  College  is  $14,043,518.  The  budget  was  to 
have  been  presented  to  the  College  Board  of  Trustees  at  their  meeting  last  Thursday  evening. 

"1  think  in  the  next  two  months,  you  will  want  to  watch  this  College  because  some  ex- 
citing things  are  going  to  happen,"  Dr.  Breuder  said,  pointing  to  campus  changes  -  including 
some  which  will  be  made  possible  by  passage  of  the  budget. 

College's  tuition  policy  is  slated 
for  update  to  per-credit  basis 

A  change  in  tuition  policy  at  the  College  has  been  announced.  The  new  system,  involving 
paying  tuition  on  a  per-credit  basis,  will  replace  the  present  system  of  fiat-rate. 

The  installation  of  the  new  tuition  system  would  eliminate  any  unfairness  regarding  pay- 
ment by  the  student,  according  to  College  officials. 

Now,  a  student  carrying  12  credits  pays  the  same  amount  as  a  student  carrying  18  credits. 
The  new  tuition  scale  would  cause  the  student  with  more  credits  to  [Jay  more  than  the  student 
with  fewer  credits,  according  to  Dr.  Robert  L.  Breuder,  College  president. 

Charges  involved  in  the  new  system  are:  sponsor  district  student,  $37.75  per  credit;  stu- 
dent sponsored  by  another  Pennsylvania  community  college,  $46.60  per  credit;  non-sponsored 
student,  $85.65  per  credit;  and  out-of-state  student,  $126  per  credit.
