Computer  Science  Club 
sweatshirt  payments 
can  be  made  now 

Students  who  signed  up  for  Computer 
Science  Club  sweatshirts  may  contact  David  A. 
Lett,  instructor  of  computer  science,  at  College 
Ext.  228  or  in  Room  305,  Academic  Center 
(ACC),  about  making  payments. 

According  to  B.  Drew  Robison,  club  public 
relations  ofUcer  and  computer  science  student 
from  Duncansville,  the  sweatshirts  will  be 
distributed  from  Monday,  April  23  to  Monday, 
April  30. 

"Persons  who  ordered  the  shirts  should  at- 
tend the  Computer  Science  Club  meeting  at  3:30 
p.m.  tomorrow  in  Room  321,  ACC.  It  is  sug- 
gested that  those  who  ordered  begin  paying  right 
away,"  said  Robison. 


Bookstore  plans 
'book-buy'  this  month 
for  used  textbooks 

The  Bookstore  will  be  offering  a  book-buy 
for  used  textbooks,  according  to  Mrs.  Eleonore 
R.  Holcomb,  Bookstore  supervisor.  Students 
may  bring  in  textbooks  that  they  wish  to  sell  on 
Thursday  and  Friday,  April  26  and  27,  from  9 
a.m.  to  12  p.m.,  and  from  2  to  4  p.m. 

On  Monday,  April  30,  the  last  day  of 
classes,  the  Bookstore  will  be  buying  back  books 
from  9  a.m.  until  12  p.m. 

From  Monday,  April  23,  until  Monday, 
April  30,  the  Bookstore  will  be  having  a  20  per- 
cent sale  on  all  items  except  textbooks  and  goods 
already  on  sale,  said  Mrs.  Holcomb. 


Get  the  high-tech  career 
you  want  when  you  want  it. 


The  Navy's  Delayed  Entry  Program  can 
guarantee  you  the  training  you  want,  when  you 
wani  it.  Qualify  and  you  choose  the  training  you 
wani  from  over  60  career  areas.  Then  you  may 
have  up  to  one  full  year  before  you  report.  That 
gives  you  lime  to  do  what  you  want  to  do  -  finish 
school,  travel,  or  just  relax.  When  you  begin  the 
Navy  adventure,  you  begin  with  training  in 
sophisticated  fields  like  computers,  electronics, 
nuclear  power  or  communications.  Then  you  gel 
important  on-the-job  experience  that  lets  you 
become  a  master  at  the  career  you  chose  while 
you  travel  to  places  like  Greece,  Spain  and  Hong 
Kong.  To  find  out  more  about  the  advantages  of 
the  Navy's  Delayed  Entry  Program,  see  your 
guidance  counselor  or  call  your  local  Navy 
represenlalive.    1-800-692-7823 


You  can  learn  tomorrow's 
technologies  in  today's  Navy.

A  dream...  The  Olmypics...  and  a  need 

"Everybody's  gel  a  dream..." 

Simply,  Scott  S.  Zimmerman  says  it. 

It's  his  reason  for  setting  out  on  a  pavement-pounding  effort  to  get  backing  to  take  part  in 
an  Olympics-related  event. 

Zimmerman  wants  to  run  one  of  the  mile  segments  in  the  Olympics  pass-the-torch,  cross- 
country run  this  spring. 

"I've  always  wanted  to  go  to  the  Olympics,"  says  Zimmerman,  adding  that,  in  a  small 
way,  the  special  event  is  a  way  to  do  that. 

The  special  event  being  staged  by  the  Olympics  Committee  involves  setting  up  4,000  run- 
ners from  across  the  nation  to  run  in  one-mile  segments  carrying  the  symbolic  torch  from  New 
England  to  Los  Angeles,  the  scene  of  the  Summer  Olympics. 

As  part  of  the  promotional  effort,  the  Committee  asks  each  would-be  participant  to  chip 
in  sponsorship  donations  of  $3,000  ~  in  keeping  with  this  year's  Olympics  Committee  effort  to 
pay  expenses  with  private  funding  and  not  secure  government  funds. 

Zimmerman,  a  broadcasting  student  at  the  College,  is  working  on  getting  that  sponsor- 
ship. A  resident  of  Milton,  Zimmerman  has  found  that  groups  at  the  high  school  he  attended 
(Milton  Area  High  School)  are  supportive:  Various  clubs  and  individuals  have  made  dona- 
tions. Obviously  -  and  he  admits  it  ~  he's  looking  for  more. 

If  Zimmerman  gets  the  needed  sponsorship  by  the  end  of  the  month,  he  says  he  probably 
will  run  a  segment  in  the  northwestern  part  of  the  slate  -  as  "slow  as  I  can"  to  keep  up  the 
schedule  but  still  savor  the  experience.

FIRST  recipients  of  the  Robert  Jessell  Memorial  Scholarship  are  Neil  J.  Pillot,   carpentry 
and  building  construction  technology  student  from  Grassflat  (left)  and  Dennis  A.  Nearhood, 
construction  carpentry  student  from  Winburne.   Presenting  the  awards  is  Charles  Fiantaca, 
chairman  of  the  building  construction  and  construction  carpentry  advisory  committee. 
Both  students  will  be  graduated  in  May.   IPhoto  courtesy  of  College  Information  Office! 

Two  students  honored;  receive 
Robert  Jessell  Memorial  Scholarship 


Courtesy  of  the  College  Information  Office 
Two  College  students  were  honored  last 
Wednesday,  at  the  annual  spring  meeting  of  the 
Building  Construction  Technology  and  Con- 
struction Carpentry  Advisory  Committee,  accor- 
ding to  Dr.  Ralph  A.  Home,  Construction 
Technology  Division  director. 

The  two  students  were  the  first  recipients  of 
the  Robert  Jessell  Memorial  Scholarship. 

Dennis  A.  Nearhood,  of  RD  2,  Box  3,  Win- 
burne, a  construction  carpentry  student,  with  a 
4.0  cumulative  grade  average,  and  Neil  J.  Pillot, 
PO  Box  212,  Grassflat,  a  carpentry  and  building 
construction  technology  student  with  a  3.8 
cumulative  grade  average,  each  received  $250. 


Presenting  the  awards  was  Charles  Fian- 
taca, chairman  of  the  Building  Construction  and 
Construction  Carpentry  Advisory  Committee. 

The  $500  for  the  scholarships  was  made 
available  by  the  West  Branch  Susquehanna 
Home  Builders  Association  of  Williamsport. 

Home  said  two  awards  will  be  given  every 
year  in  the  amount  of  $250  each  to  a  student  in 
construction  carpentry  and  a  student  in  carpen- 
try and  building  construction  technology. 

The  awards  take  into  consideration  the 
students'  cumulative  grade  average,  personal  at- 
titude, and  potential  for  success  after  gradua- 
tion. 

Both  students  will  be  graduated  in  May. 


2     .     SPOTLIGHT    â€˘     (Monday.  April  16,  1984 

Associate  professor 
offered  membership 

Mrs.  Vivian  P.  Moon,  associate  professor 
of  food  service,  has  been  invited  to  be  a  member 
of  the  delegation  of  dietitians  selected  by  the 
Citizen  Ambassador  Program  of  People  to  Peo- 
ple. 

The  purpose  of  the  People  to  People  pro- 
gram is  one  of  "creating  goodwill  and 
understanding  between  Americans  and  the 
citizens  of  other  countries,"  said  Ms.  Donna  R. 
Watson,  associate  professor  and  director  of  ad- 
minsitrative  dietetic  internship. 

The  dietetic  delegation  will  study  the  nutri- 
tional status,  dietary  patterns,  food  products  and 
nutritional  care  and  delivery  systems  of  Australia 
and  New  Zealand,  according  to  Ms.  Watson, 

Mrs.  Moon  said  she  has  regretfully  turned 
down  the  appointment  but  said  she  would  like  to 
go  another  year  and  hopes  she  is  asked  again. 

Secondary  division 
to  undergo  audit 

The  Secondary  Division  of  the  College  will 
undergo  a  compliance  audit  from  today  through 
this  Thursday,  April  19. 

According  to  Michael  J.  Stanzione,  secon- 
dary vocational  program  coordinator  at  the  Col- 
lege, a  team  of  auditors  from  the  Pennsylvania 
Department  of  Education  will  be  checking  for 
compliance  with  state  regulations  in  the  voca- 
tional education  program. 

The  team,  co-chaired  by  Kenneth  Adams 
and  Ms.  Glendean  Davis,  will  look  for  com- 
pliance in  areas  such  as  discrimination  (racial  or 
sexual),  facilities  for  the  handicapped,  and  the 
upholding  of  safety  standards,  said  Stanzione. 

This  review  is  done  on  a  five-year  rotating 
basis.  A  report  is  expected  in  30  days,  he  said. 

Grades/transcripts 

will  be  held 
if  tickets  not  paid 

Grades  and/or  transcripts  will  not  be  sent 
out  to  those  students  with  parking  tickets  not  set- 
tled by  next  Friday,  April  27,  in  the  Security  Of- 
fice, according  to  Lawrence  P.  Smeak,  super- 
visor of  security. 


Personnel  changes 
are  approved 
by  trustees 

Personnel  changes  were  voted  upon  at  the 
April  meeting  of  the  College  Board  of  Trustees, 
on  Thursday,  April  5. 

Four  resignations  were  approved  by  the 
board.   Those  resigning  include: 

Frank  P.  Leach,  assistant  professor  of  air 
conditioning  and  refrigeration,  effective  Aug.l9. 
Leach  has  been  .aiployed  by  the  College  since 
August  1977. 

Nancy  K.  Lockridge,  regular  part-time 
receptionist/clerk  typist,  warehouse,  effective 
March  29. 

Robert  A.  Patton,  instructor  of  dairy  herd 
management  at  the  Earth  Science  Campus,  effec- 
tive at  the  end  of  the  1983-84  school  year. 

Craig  V.  Shaffer,  night  custodian,  general 
services,  effective  March  16. 

Professional  employment  changes  were  also 
approved  by  the  board. 

Kathy  Dumanis  will  continue  as  temporary 
full-time  replacement  instructor  of  economics, 
Integrated  Studies  Division  through  May  12,  at 
an  annual  172-day  salary  of  $19,530,  to  be  pro- 
rated. Ms.  Dumanis  replaces  Harvey  H.  Kuhns, 
who  is  on  a  leave  of  absence  without  pay. 

Nancy  C.  Elias  was  granted  a  disability 
leave  of  absence  without  pay,  effective  Dec.  20, 
1983  through  June  30,  1984. 

Harvey  H.  Kuhns,  associate  professor  of 
economics,  was  granted  an  extension  on  a  leave 
of  absence  without  pay  through  June  30,  or  the 
effective  date  of  his  retirement. 

Mrs.  Davie  Jane  Nestarick  will  continue  as 
coordinator  of  the  dental  hygiene  program,  ef- 
fective July  1,  1984,  at  an  annual  205-day  salary 
of  $22,899,  plus  a  to-be-determined  increment. 
Mrs.  Nestarick  has  been  employed  as  acting 
coordinator  since  Aug.  1,  1983. 

Lenore  G.  Penfield,  was  granted  a  disability 
leave  of  absence  without  pay,  effective  tentatively 
March  21  through  April  27.  She  will  resume 
duties  as  regular  instructor  of  dental  hygiene,  ef- 
fective Aug.  20,  at  an  annual  salary  of  $17,429, 
plus  a  to-be-determined  increment.  Mrs.  Pen- 
field  replaces  Mrs.  Nestarick,  who  transferred  to 
the  coordinator's  position. 


Monday.  April  16,  1984     â€˘     SPOTLIGHT     â€˘     3 


Two  educational  representatives 

from  Taiwan,  Republic  of  China 

visit  College;  give  views 


By  Robert  0.  Hawk 
Of  The  SPOTLIGHT  Staff 

Two  educational  representatives  from  the 
Republic  of  China  -  I.  Fan  Chang,  Ph.  D., 
director  of  Technical  and  Vocational  Education 
in  the  Ministry  of  Education  for  Taiwan,  and 
Clifford  S.  N.  Yoh,  president  of  National  Tai 
Chang  Institute  of  Commerce,  Republic  of  China 
--  visited  the  College  in  late  March. 

They  took  part  in  a  question  and  answer 
session  with  The  SPOTLIGHT.  The  interview 
was  held  in  the  president's  conference  room. 

In  opening  comments,  Yoh  mentioned  his 
warm  regard  for  Dr.  Robert  L.  Breuder,  College 
president,  and  mentioned  that  they  had  been 
close  personal  friends  for  seven  years. 
A  good  community  college 

Expressing  his  reaction  to  the  College, 
Chang  said,  "We  have  seen  a  good  community 
college  which  serves  the  community  well." 

Giving  some  background  about  the  educa- 
tional system  of  Taiwan,  Dr.  Chang  explained 
that  their  system  begins  with  nine  years  of  com- 
pulsory instruction  and,  for  the  remaining  three 
years  of  high  school,  a  vocational  or  academic 
course  of  study. 

"After  this,"  he  continued,  "students  have 
the  option  of  going  on  to  receive  a  three-year 
associate  degree  or  a  four-year  degree  which  can 
be  followed  by  a  doctorate  program  if  desired." 

Agricultural,  technical  and  academic 

There  are  77  junior  colleges  in  the  Republic 
of  China  -  "none  of  which  we  particularly  want 
to  change,"  said  Chang.  "We  have  agricultural 
colleges,  technical  colleges  and  academic  col- 
leges. Our  island  is  not  very  big  and  we  have 
enough  colleges  which  make  up  divisions  of  one 
big  community  college." 

Chang  commented,  "We  do  lack  your  col- 
lege's open  access/open  entry  policies." 

There  are  300,000  graduates  from  the  ninth 
grade  every  year  in  the  republic.  Fifty  percent 
continue  with  vocational  education  and  50  per- 


cent continue  with  academic  education. 

The  island  of  Taiwan,  which  has  a  national 
population  of  18,000,000  people,  has  a  two  per- 
cent unemployment  rate. 

80  percent  remain  in  U.S. 

Five  thousand  university  graduates  come  to 
the  United  States  for  advanced  educational  train- 
ing every  year;  20  percent  of  them  return  to 
Taiwan  leaving  the  remaining  80  percent  conti- 
nuing to  reside  in  the  United  Stales. 

There  is  no  mandatory  requirement  for 
students  obtaining  further  education  here  to 
return  to  the  Republic  of  China  for  some  set  time 
of  service. 

However,  if  students  do  wish  to  return, 
there  is  a  national  committee  which  will  provide 
them  with  a  plane  ticket  and  job  placement 
assistance  upon  their  arrival  back  home. 

Chang  added,  "In  technical  fields,  at  least 
half  of  the  instructors  are  trained  in  the  United 
States."  Dr.  Chang  was  educated  at  Cornell 
University. 

...no  'sweet  talks' 

In  a  comment  concerning  overtures  made 
by  their  much  larger  neighbor,  the  People's 
Republic  of  China,  earlier  this  year,  Chang  and 
Yoh  were  in  intense  agreement  that  "there  is  no 
way  we,  as  a  people,  can  return  to  the  People's 
Republic  of  China.  From  past  experience  with 
Communists,  we  do  not  believe  their  sweet 
talks." 

Yoh  continued,  "Tibet  is  an  example. 
They    asked    Tibet     to     voluntarily    annex 
themselves  with  promises  that  there  would  be 
nothing  done  to  lessen  religious  freedom  or 
freedom  of  speech. 

Tibet  agreed  and,  as  soon  as  this  was  ac- 
complished, things  changed  very  quickly  for  the 
worse.  "No,  to  the  contrary,  we  invite  Ihem  to 
return  to  our  system  of  government." 

Both  men  ended  their  comments  with  praise 
for  the  efforts  being  made  at  the  College  and 
thanked  both  students  and  faculty  for  the 
hospitality  they  received  during  their  visit.

Monday,  April  16,  1984     â€˘     SPOTLIGHT 

Ronstadt's  What's  New? 
is  treat  to  tired  ears 

Music  review  by  Robert  O.  Hawk 

Linda  Ronstadt's  newest  album.  What's  New?  is  a  blend  ol  standard  big-band  ballads 
and  the  inimilable  style  of  an  artist  who  has,  without  question,  evolved  into  a  truly  superior 
songstress  and  stylist. 

From  her  days  as  the  lead  vocalist  for  her  band.  The  Stone  Ponies  (circa  1968-1973), 
Ronstadt  has  given  power-packed  performances  ranging  from  rock-and-roll  to  country 
music. 

Since  finding  her  niche,  so  to  speak,  as  a  solo  artist  with  album  producer  Peter  Asher 
(formerly  of  Peter  &  Gordon),  she  has  developed  a  vocal  style  which  has  become  a 
trademark  all  her  own. 

This  style  has  been  put  nicely  together  in  her  newest  album  in  which  she  teams  up  with 
The  Nelson  Riddle  Orchestra. 

Riddle  is  a  master  of  the  theme  song  and  romantic  ballad.  His  work  with  Ronstadt  lends 
his  expertise  to  an  accomplished  vocalist.  All  the  way  around,  the  album  is  a  compliment  to 
both. 

From  the  title  song.  What's  New?,  to  Someone  Who'll  Watch  Over  Me  -  which  are  two 
of  my  favorites  out  of  an  album  of  songs  -  it  is  hard  to  say  which  is  the  best  song  of  all. 

To  my  mind,  this  is  an  album  that  Is  a  treat  to  tired  ears  and  is  a  fresh  look  at  some  songs 
which  have  stood  the  test  of  time. 


A  Special  Thank  You 

As  the  academic  year  rapidly  nears  its  end.  The  SPOTLIGHT  thanks  ail  those 
who  have  in  a  variety  of  ways  contributed  to  our  efforts  during  the  past  months. 

Among  those,  of  course,  are  our  advertisers  who  recognized  our  special  market 
but  also  recognized  that  our  efforts  are  in  the  nature  of  a  learning  experience. 

Among  those,  too,  are  all  members  of  the  College  community,  including 
students,  faculty,  administrators,  secretaries  and  other  staff,  the  College  security 
force,  the  switchboard  personnel,  the  custodial  staff. . .  And.  our  production  "squad" 
at  The  Milton  Standard...  And.  the  Journalism  Advisory  Committee, ,  And  staffers  at 
the  various  local  and  area  newspapers  who  gave  suggestions  and  reactions...  and.. 

...And  so  many  people  --  individuals  â– â–   who  offered  criticism,  reaction,  sugges- 
tions, comments. 

To  say  more  would  be  maudlin:  We  simply  say... 

THANK  YOU! 
â– â– The  Staff  and  The  Adviser.  The  SPOTLIGHT 


6     â€˘     SPOTLIGHT     â€˘     Monday,  Aprill  6,  1984 

Hill  Street  Blues 

a  microcosm  of  urban 

strife  and  realism 

Television  review 

By 
Mark  S.  Schwanke 

While  Irekking  across  thai  barren 
wasteland  called  television,  I  found  an  oasis  in 
the  form  of  Hill  Street  Blues. 

This  progrann  about  life  in  an  inner  city 
police  precinct  deals  with  sensitive  topics  with 
a  gritty  realism. 

For  example,  a  policeman  enters  a  run- 
down building  after  being  called  about  a  family 
disturbance  Silhouetted  against  the  wall  is  the 
form  of  someone  holding  a  gun.  The  shadow 
moves,  so  the  cop  discharges  his  weapon.  A 
small  black  child  with  a  toy  pistol  falls,  mortally 
wounded 

The  cop  does  not  go  on  with  his  life  as 
though  nothing  had  happened.  The  child's 
mother  does  not  forgive  the  cop,  saying  that  he 
was  just  trying  to  do  a  difficult  job  There  is  ten- 
sion, hate,  anger,  and  the  examination  of  a 
policeman's  use  of  lethal  force. 

This  verity  is  carried  out  in  a  large  but 
strong  cast  of  relatively  unknown  actors.  The 
most  visible  are  precinct  Captain  Frank  Furillo 
(Daniel  J.  Travanti);  his  wife,  cool  public  defen- 
dent  Joyce  Davenport  Furillo  (Veronica  Hamel); 
scruffy  detective  Mick  Belker  (Bruce  Weitz), 

eloquent  Lieutenant  Howard  Hunter  (James  B. 
Sikking),  and  Sergeant  Lucy  Bates  (Betty 
Thomas). 

Hill  Street  Blues  is  frenetically  paced  and 
violent;  it  is  a  microcosm  of  urban  strife.  The 
turbulence  of  life  for  a  street  cop  is  dealt  with  in 
a  forceful  and  intensive  manner.  This  show  is 
on  late  in  the  evening,  but  it  will  never  lull  one  to 
sleep 


Reviewer  peers 
at  recent  singles 

Music  review 

By 

James  K.  Morrissey 

The  generally  limited  playlists  of  the  televi- 
sion music  video  shows  and  the  Top  40  for- 
mats of  the  the  local  commercial  radio  stations 
do  not  give  much  attention  to  progressive  ar- 
tists in  the  world  of  music. 

Given  a  chance  to  be  different,  local  col- 
lege radio  stations  often  end  up  with  similar  for- 
mats. How  many  times  have  you  heard  /  Want 
A  New  Drug  by  Huey  Lewis  and  the  News  or 
Hold  Me  Now  by  the  Thompson  Twins?  Ten 
million  times? 

It's  all  a  matter  of  taste  anyway,  so  this 
reviewer  will  get  off  the  pulpit  and  switch  from 
sermons  to  looking  at  some  fairly  recent 
singles. 

Head  Over  Heels  (Go-Go's)  -  Although  the 
lyrics  are  about  being  "out  of  sync"  and  "out  of 
control,"  it's  actually  a  very  controlled  effort 
from  these  bubbleheads.  The  song  is  catchy 
and  has  good  keyboards  and  bass.  This  may 
be  a  number  one  single  very  soon. 

Automatic  (Pointer  Sisters)  -  Whenever 
this    song    is    broadcast    on    the    radio,    the 
response  is  to  turn  it  off  without  thinking. 
There's    no    way    to    control    it;    it's    totally 
automatic. 

68  Guns  (The  Alarm)  -  Quite  a  few  critics 
are  raving  about  this  band  On  closer  inspec- 
tion, they're  just  a  watered-down  imitation  of 
the  early  Clash's  rebellious  punk  stance.  This 
alarm  has  gone  off  eight  years  too  late  to  ring 
much  excitement  in  these  jaded  ears. 

Run  Runaway  (Slade)  -  From  1971-1975, 
Blade  racked  up  13  Top  10  singles  in  Britain. 
Six  of  these,  including  the  original  Cum  On  Feel 
The  Noize,  went  to  number  one.  This  song, 
although  nothing  special,  might  give  Slade  their 
first  hit  in  America. 


Repayment  of  Emergency  Loans 

A  reminder  from  the  Financial  Aid  Office 
Students  are  reminded  that  outstanding  G.  Walter  Smith  loans  (up  to  $30)  and  larger  emergency  \oansmust 
be  repaid  by  the  end  of  the  semester. 

For  those  students  who  have  not  repaid  the  loan  at  that  time,  a  hold  will  be  placed  on  all  College  records  and 
no  transcript,  diploma,  or  other  information  will  be  released  until  the  financial  obligation  has  been  satisfied. 

Students  should  check  with  the  Financial  Aid  Office  if  they  are  not  sure  whether  or  not  they  have  an 
outstanding  loan. 

â– â–   Donald  S.  Shade,  director  of  financial  aid

Monday,  April  16,  1984 


Two  College  staff  members 
attend  one-day  conference 


"Community  Problem  Solving  Through  Community  Education"  was  the  topic  of  a  one- 
day  conference  at  the  Montgomery  County  Community  College  on  Friday,  April  6,  according 
to  Ms.  Sandra  L.  Rosenberger,  coordinator  of  community-based  activities. 

Ms.  Rosenberger  and  Grant  L.  Martin,  coordinator  of  specialized  technical  educational 
programs,  met  with  their  counterpails  from  other  community  colleges  at  the  conference. 

"One  hundred  fifty  people  attended.  Most  of  them  were  from  Philadelphia;  we  traveled 
the  longest  distance  to  get  there,"  said  Ms.  Rosenberger. 
Local  dislricis  involved 

Ms.  Rosenberger  attended  two  workshops  throughout  the  day.  One  was  called  "How  to 
Start  an  After-School  Care  Program  in  Your  Public  School". 

"Most  involved  were  from  the  local  school  districts,"  Ms.  Rosenberger  said.  "I'd  like  to 
start  programs  for  latch-key  children  -  the  ones  whose  parents  work  and  they  come  home  all 
alone  -  but  I  don't  think  it  will  be  feasible  to  do  on  a  college  level." 

The  second  workshop  Ms.  Rosenberger  attended  was  called  "Doing  More  with  Less".  A. 
William  Sabatino,  executive  director  of  Community  Accountants,  Norristown,  spoke  about  the 
importance  of  planning  and  organization. 

"It  basically  showed  how  to  do  a  lot  of  programs  without  spending  a  lot  of  money,"  Ms. 
Rosenberger  said. 

Martin  attended  two  workshops  called  "Community  Resources  Information  Bank"  and 
"A  Network  of  Pennsylvania's  Industry-Education  Coordination  Projects". 
Focus  on  problem-solving 

According  to  both  Martin  and  Ms.  Rosenberger,  the  lunch-time  lecture  was  very  in- 
teresting. 

Dr.  Russell  L.  Ackolf,  Silberberg  Professor  of  Systems  Sciences,  Wharton  School  of  the 
University  of  Pennsylvania  gave  a  lecture  on  problem-solving. 

"He  gave  a  new  approach  to  problem  solving,"  said  Martin.  "Rather  than  looking  at 
each  problem  in  a  system,  you  look  at  the  system  as  a  whole." 

Ms.  Rosenberger  described  Dr.  Ackoffs  speech  as  "solving  a  problem  backwards". 

She  added,  "It's  just  like  a  car.  If  you  take  the  best  parts  of  each  car  -  say  an  engine 
from  a  Mercedes,  a  muffler  from  a  Chevy,  a  steering  wheel  from  a  Volvo  -  you  should  have 
the  ideal  parts  to  create  the  best  car.  But  when  you  try  to  put  them  together,  you're  not  go- 
ing to  have  a  very  good  car,  because  they  won't  fit  togehter." 

Martin  and  Ms.  Rosenberger  noted  that  Dr.  Ackoff  is  also  involved  in  the  technological 
world. 

According  to  Martin,  "He  (Dr.  Ackoff)  was  instrumental  in  the  development  of  the  touch- 
tone  telephone."

Monday.  April  16,  1984     â€˘     SPOTLIGHT     â€˘     9 


College  trustees  look 
toward  major  changes 


The  monthly  College  Board  of  Trustees 
meeting  was  held  Thursday,  April  5  in  the 
Automotive  Trades  Center. 

Mario  Caldera,  board  chairman,  and 
Thomas  C.  Large,  architect,  presented  an  update 
report  on  the  College. 

"Enough  cannot  be  said  about  the  stupen- 
dous changes  in  the  complexion  of  the  campus  -- 
to  the  College's  credit,"  commented  Caldera. 

Several  architect's  renderings  of  the  campus 
were  presented  the  board.  The  drawings  showed 
various  stages  of  advancement  during  the  next 
lO-year  period. 

Eventually,  according  to  Large,  the  plan  in- 
cludes "closing  in"  the  campus,  allowing  for  a 
"green  area"  beyond  the  Building  Trades 
Center,  toward  the  railroad  crossing. 

Eventually,  roads  will  be  added,  according 
to  the  plan,  to  conned  Park  Street  and  another 
road  to  be  added  west  of  campus.  The  plan 
shows  the  eventual  closing  in  of  the  College  cam- 
pus, complete  with  pedestrian  paths  replacing 
the  existing  Susquehanna  Street 

Electrical  system  planned 

Plans  to  be  completed  in  the  near  future  in- 
clude installation  of  a  new  electrical  system  in 
the  Academic  Center,  in  addition  to  the  comple- 
tion of  renovations  to  the  Technical  Trades 
buildings. 

In  other  business,  among  bid  approvals,  the 
purchase  of  the  new  Computer  Assisted  Drafting 
and  Design  and  Computer  Assisted  Manufactur- 
ing (CADD/CAM)  was  presented  to  the  board 
for  approval.  The  system  will  aid  existing  pro- 
grams of  the  College,  such  as  graphic  arts,  by 
allowing  students  to  design  on  a  computer 
screen. 

Dr.  Robert  L.  Breuder,  College  president, 
commented,  "This  new  system  will  help  launch 
the  College  into  the  future  and  make  our 
students  more  competitive  in  the  job  market." 

The  system,  provided  by  International 
Business  Machines  (IBM)  and  costing  $569,000 
was  approved.  Plans  to  have  it  operational  by 
this  Fall  have  been  made. 

The  board  also  approved  the  1984/85  Col- 
lege budget  proposal,  as  presented  by  Dr. 
Breuder.    The  new  budget  calls  for  a  total  of 


$16.7  million,  representing  a  7.9  percent  increase 
over  last  year's  budget. 

Dr.  Daniel  J.  Doyle,  professor  of  govern- 
ment and  history,  and  Rodney  G.  Hurley,  dean 
of  educational  research,  presented  a  report  to  the 
board  regarding  the  Middle  States  evaluation 
team.  As  part  of  the  report,  they  presented  the 
long-range  planning  report  for  the  College, 
which  was  given  to  the  accreditation  team  for 
their  consideration. 

The  announcement  was  made  that  the  next 
meeting  of  the  Board  of  Trustees  will  be  held  on 
Monday,  April  30,  rather  than  the  previously 
stated  May  7  meeting. 


Biology  Club  car 
wash  this  Saturday 

The  Biology  Club  will  sponsor  a  car 
wash  this  Saturday  to  raise  money  for  the 
club. 

It  will  be  held  at  the  Bonanza  Steak 
House  parking  lot.  The  cost  per  car  will  be 
$2.  Time  will  be  from  1  to  3  p.m.,  accor- 
ding to  Miss  Sharon  A.  Souter,  member  of 
the  Biology  Club.

Monday,  April  16,  1984     â€˘     SPOTLIGHT     â€˘ 

Dental  hygiene  students 

learn  in  many  ways  -  including 

working  daily  with  the  public 

Courtesy  College  litformalion  Office 

College  students  learn  in  many  ways...  in  the  classroom,  in  the  lab,  in  actual  work  set- 
tings. Dental  hygiene  students  are  no  exception--they  utilize  all  of  these  learning  methods. 

The  dental  hygiene  lab  is  located  on  the  fourth  floor  of  the  Academic  Center.  What 
makes  their  program  unique  is  that  they  work  with  the  public,  everyday,  in  a  learning  environ- 
ment. 

The  Dental  Hygiene  Clinic  is  open  to  the  public  from  8  a.m.  to  4:30  p.m.,  Monday 
through  Friday. 

During  these  hours,  preventive  dental  services  are  provided. 

About  50  persons  a  week  take  advantage  of  these  servics  -  especially  for  oral  prophylaxis 
(teeth  cleaning).  Other  services  provided  include  flouride  treatment,  x-rays,  patient  education, 
and  nutritional  counseling. 

Small  fee  charged 

According  to  Mrs.  Davie  Jane  Nestarick,  dental  hygiene  interim  coordinator,  a  nominal 
fee  is  charged  to  help  defray  the  cost  of  materials. 

Medical  assistant  cards  are  accepted  in  the  clinic.  Persons  who  want  to  make  an  appoint- 
ment should  call  (717)  326-3761,  Ext.  407.  Those  having  work  done  at  the  clinic  should  allow 
extra  time  as  students  are  in  a  learning  situation  and  will  take  longer  to  do  the  work  required, 
she  said. 

The  students  are  supervised  by  the  dental  hygiene  instructor  and  a  dentist  who  is  always 
present. 

Six  dentists  from  Lycoming  County  supervise  at  the  clinic  on  an  alternating  basis.  The 
dentists  examine  each  patient  and  supervise  students'  work.

Franklin  J.  Seber  rolls  278; 
CU-JO  clinches  league  title 


One...  two...  three...  strikes,  you're  out. 

Of  course  it  is  the  baseball  season  for  many 
but  three  strikes  for  league  bowler  Franklin  J. 
Seber,  electrical  technology  student  from  Scran- 
Ion  almost  led  to  a  perfect  game. 

Going  into  the  ninth  frame,  it  seemed  as 
though  everyone  in  the  league  was  holding  their 
breath  for  him.  But  when  Seber  rolled  the  ball 
down  the  lane,  he  unfortunately  left  just  one  pin 
standing. 

Seber  bowls  for  CU-JO  -  the  team  which 
has  clinched  the  tide  in  intramural  bowling. 

Other  results  according  to  records  provided 
by  ABC  Bowling  Lanes:

Monday,  April  16,  1984 


One  of  the  season's  best  athletes: 
Sutton  holds  promising  future 


QUIET  ON  THE  COURT...  Well,  it's  not 
exactly  a  movie  production.  In  fact,  it  is  a 
reference  to  peaceful  player  Richard  A.  Sutton, 
carpentry  construction  student  from  Towanda 
who  played  basketball  for  the  College's  Wildcat 
basketball  team. 

"His  only  fault  is  the  fact  that  he  is  too 
quiet  and  for  that  reason,  I  could  never  see  him 
(Sutton)  as  a  point  guard.  But,  on  the  other 
hand,  his  exquisite  ability  makes  him  a  fine 
shooting  guard,"  commented  Louis  J.  Menago, 
who  also  is  machine  shop  instructor. 
Good  all-round  player 

"He  is  not  just  a  fine  outside  shooter,  but 
he  is  a  superb  rebounder  for  his  height  (6'  I")  as 
well  as  a  tenacious  defensive  player,"  added 
Menago. 

Sutton's  game  high  of  hte  season  was  35 
points  -  -  scored  against  Lycoming  College's  JV 
team.  Throughout  the  season,  he  scored  31 
points  against  P.S.U.  Schuylkill  Campus;  netted 
29  points  against  the  Community  College  of 
Philadelphia  and  also  against  Lehigh  County 
Community  College,  and  sunk  13  baskets  for  a 
total  of  26  points  against  Keystone  Junior  Col- 


"He  was  a  model  player  to  work  with 
because  he  would  always  give  all  that  he  had. 
He  is  a  very  unselfish  type  of  player  and  a  very 
coachable  individual  --  which  from  a  coach's 
standpoint  is  one  of  the  most  desirerable 
characteristics,"  stated  Menago. 

Menago  feels  conndenl 

"He  probably  increased  his  potential  100 
percent  from  last  year  and  this  is  due  to  his  in- 
tensity and  confidence.  I  feel  positive  that,  with 
a  diligent  effort  on  his  part  to  work  hard  over  the 
summer  months,  he  should  be  able  to  fit  into  a 
major  college  basketball  program,"  stated 
Menago. 

Future  outlook  good 

Menago,  whose  own  basketball  career  was 
hampered  by  knee  injuries,  began  coaching  at 
the  College  three  years  ago.  He  feels  rather  con- 
fident and  state  that  although  it  takes  a  few  years 
to  get  a  program  established,  it  looks  like  the 
potential  is  increasing  yearly. 

In  the  coach's  eyes  "it  takes  more  fellows 
like  Sutton  to  get  them  on  their  feet  and  keep 
them  there." 


Arm  wrestling  tournament  tonight; 
students  -  men  and  women  -  asked 
to  take  part,  register  right  away 


Students  interested  in  participating  a 
"Monster  Arm  Wrestling  Tournament"  may

and  College  activities.

Fremiotti.

p.m.

Cross-country  runners 
'sign  up'  for  Fall  semester 
now  underway 

Students  interested  in  intercollegiate  cross- 
country running  competition  in  the  fall  may  con- 
tact Phillip  D.  Landers,  associate  professor  of 
business  administration,  at  College  Ext.  227,  or 
in  Room  307,  Academic  Center  (ACC). 

"Both  men  and  women  are  encouraged  to 
join,"  said  Landers.  "I  would  like  the  names 
and  addresses  of  interested  persons  so  I  can  keep 
in  touch  with  them  over  the  summer.  The  cross- 
country season  begins  the  first  week  of  Fall 
semester."

Middle  States  Team 
duties  completed 


LRP  group  'feels  good' 
about  evaluator's  visit 

The  Middle  States  Association  Evaluation 
Team  wound  up  its  three-day  visit  to  the  College 
last  Wednesday,  according  to  Rodney  G. 
Hurley,  dean  of  educational  research,  planning 
and  evaluation. 

"We  feel  good  about  the  visit,"  said 
Hurley,  who  added  that  the  long  range  planning 
process  administrators  won't  know  the  final 
results  of  the  visit  until  the  Middle  States 
Association  reacts  to  the  report. 

Last  Monday  through  Wednesday,  the 
ll-member  team  toured  the  Main,  North,  Earth 
Science,  and  Aviation  Campuses,  as  well  as  the 
Danville  State  Farm  facility.  Evaluators  talked 
to  students  and  faculty  members  randomly,  ask- 
ing questions  about  courses,  policies  and  College 
procedures. 

The  team  also  met  with  every  College  ad- 
ministrative office.  Four  evaluators  lunched 
with  Student  Government  Association  officers 
(SGA)  and  other  student  leaders. 

To  end  the  visit,  team  leader  Dr.  Robert  L. 
Gell  gave  an  "exit  report"  to  committee 
members  who  represented  all  facets  of  the  Col- 
lege. This  will  be  turned  into  written  form  and 
given  to  College  president  Dr.  Robert  L. 
Breuder. 

After  appropriate  administrators  react  to 
this  report,  it  will  be  returned  to  Dr.  Gell,  who 
will  submit  it  to  the  Middle  States  Association 
for  the  final  reaction. 

"I  feel  the  team  has  viewed  the  College  in  a 
very  positive  light,"  said  Hurley.  He  said  many 
College  faculty  and  staff  members  have  been 
very  dedicated  throughout  the  entire  long  range 
planning  process. 

The  final  draft  of  the  long  range  planning 
should  be  completed  by  June  15,  and  given  to 
the  general,  executive,  and  administrative  coun- 
cils. Hurley  said.  This  final  draft  will  then  be 
presented  before  the  Board  of  Tnistees  at  its 
meeting  in  August. 


August  graduates 
may  petition 
after  registration 

August  graduates  may  begin  to  petition  to 
graduate  as  soon  as  they  are  officially  registered 
(paid)  for  summer  scfiool,  said  Mrs.  Therese  A. 
Keen,  student  records. 

The  last  day  for  August  graduates  to  peti- 
tion is  June  22.  All  summer  diplomas  will  show 
a  graduation  date  of  Aug.  17,  1984,  she  said. 

A  final  transcript  and  diploma  will  be  mail- 
ed to  students  after  Aug.  17.  Students  who 
desire  a  diploma  will  be  charged  a  fee  of  $S  if 
they  petition  before  June  22,  she  added. 

Students  who  petition  after  June  22  will  pay 
a  $10  fee  and  there  will  be  a  delay  in  receiving 
the  diploma,  she  stated. 

Women's  Week  activities 
discussed  at  Women's  Forum 

Women's  Week  was  the  topic  of  discussion 
at  the  Women's  Forum  meeting  last  Monday, 
April  9,  in  Room  103,  Academic  Center,  accor- 
ding to  Mrs.  Veronica  M.  Muzic,  professor  of 
English. 

"We  had  a  brief  discussion,  but  made  no 
decision  on  the  shape  of  Women's  Week.  We 
talked  about  continuing  the  series  for  next 
year,"  Mrs.  Muzic  said.  "We  also  had  a  discus- 
sion to  review  material  we  hope  to  mail  to  new 
students  to  familiarize  them  with  the  Women's 
Forum  organization." 

Mrs.  Muzic  also  noted  that  the  club 
adopted  a  logo  (identifying  symbol). 


Monday,  April  16,  1984     â€˘     SPOTLIGHT    â€˘     is 

College  to  co-sponsor 
Elderhostel  program 

The  College  in  conjunction  with  Lycoming 
College  will  co-sponsor  the  Elderhostel  program 
to  be  held  on  the  Lycoming  College  campus,  ac- 
cording to  Ms.  Sandra  L.  Rosenberger,  coor- 
dinator of  community-based  activities. 

The  program  will  be  in  two  sessions.  One  is 
from  June  17  to  23;  the  second  Is  from  Aug.  12 
to  18.  Only  those  people  60  years  old  or  older 
or  those  who  have  a  spouse  or  companion  who 
qualifies  may  attend  the  program,  said  Ms. 
Rosenberger. 

Among  courses  in  the  June  session  are 
studies  on  "Pennsylvania  Canals",  "Geology  of 
the  Appalachian  Mountains",  and  "The  Mean- 
ing of  Life". 

Among  courses  In  the  August  session  are 
"The  Best  of  Nouvelle  Cuisine",  "Baroque 
Painting",  and  "Lakes  and  Ponds-What's 
Really  In  Them". 

So  far,  26  persons  have  signed  up  for  each 
session.  They  will  come  from  all  over  the 
Eastern  Coast  to  attend  these  sessions:  Ohio, 
Maryland,  Delaware,  New  York,  West  Virginia 
North  Carolina,  New  Jersey,  Florida  and  Penn- 
sylvania, she  commented. 

Commuters  will  be  accepted  on  a  limited 
basis,  said  Ms.  Rosenberger.  Those  who  wish  to 
participate  in  the  program  as  a  commuter  should 
register  as  soon  as  possible,  she  said. 

The  deadlines  are  May  21  for  the  June  ses- 
sion and  July  16  for  the  August  session.  In- 
terested persons  may  register  in  Room  102,  the 
office  of  the  Center  for  Lifelong  Education  at  the 
College  or  by  calling  326-3727,  according  to  Ms. 
Rosenberger.

Randy  Allison  from  the  Door 
Fellowship  Church  will  be  a  guest  speaker

tomorrow  at  7  p.m.,  in  Room  6,  Academic

"dating",  according  to  Steven  R.  Baney, 
Individual  studies  student  and  member  of 
the  group.

Radiologic  technology  students 
pass  ARRT  board  examination 


Eight  members  of  the  1983  graduating  class 
were  eligible  to  lake  the  American  Registry  of 
Radiologic  Technologists  board  examination 
(ARRT)  and  some  have  already  been  employed 
in  their  field. 

The  eight  students  who  have  passed  the 
ARRT  board  examination  are  Miss  Kathy  0. 
Culton,  Miss  Heidi  D.  Fought,  Ms.  Peggy  C. 
Gunter,  Miss  Colleen  K.  Harrel,  Michael  S. 
Klesh,  Miss  Kim  M.  Shellman,  Miss  Lori  L. 
Stiger  and  Craig  C.  Pursel. 

Miss  Culton  and  Klesh  are  presently  atten- 
ding Geisinger  Medical  Center's  School  of 
Radiation  Therapy. 

Miss  Fought  and  Miss  Stiger  are  both 
employed  at  Divine  Providence  Hospital  as  staff 
radiographers. 

Miss  Harrell  is  employed  at  a  hospital  in 
Lousiana  as  a  staff  radiographer/CT  scanner 
operator.

'Safety  Seminar' 
conducted  by  FAA 
at  Aviation  Campus 

The  Federal  Aviation  Agency  (FAA)  con- 
ducted a  "Safety  Seminar"  on  Wednesday, 
April  4,  at  the  Aviation  Campus  at  the 
Williamsport-Lycoming  County  Airport  at  Mon- 
toursville,  according  to  Robert  L.  Norton,  in- 
structor aviation. 

The  seminar  went  from  12:30  to  3  p.m.  All 
first  and  second-year  students  as  well  as  the 
secondary  vocational  students  were  at  the 
seminar,  stated  Norton. 

Warren  Green,  safety  specialist,  discussed 
topics  concerning  propeller  safety,  aircraft  fuel 
safety  and  fuel  contaminatin,  aircraft  structural 
safety,  and  ground  handling  safety,  stated  Nor- 
ton. 

Daniel  Moyer,  maintenance  specialist, 
assisted  in  discussing  FAA  regulations  a 
mechanic  follows  when  he  maintains  an  aircraft. 
Problems  which  have  ocurred  recently  in 
maintenance  as  well  as  an  update  in  rules  and 
regulations  were  discussed,  Norton  also  stated. 


Miss  Shellman  is  employed  at  Bloomsburg 
Hospital  as  a  staff  radiographer. 

Pursel  is  employed  at  the  Williamsport 
Hospital  as  a  staff  radiographer. 

Out  of  the  16  students  enrolled  in  the 
radiologic  technology  1984  graduating  class, 
three  have  been  employed  as  part-time  student 
radiographers  in  the  Williamsport  Hospital's 
radiology  department. 

The  students  employed  are  William  Bannon 
of  Wellsboro;  Miss  Kim  R.  Davis  of  Wellsboro, 
and  Miss  Laura  A.  Rexer  of  Canton. 

These  students  work  during  the  3  p.m.  to  1 1 
p.m.  shift  and  during  the  day  and  evening  shifts 
on  weekends. 

They  perform  the  same  routine 
radiographer's  procedures  as  the  staff 
radiographers  and  are  under  the  direction  of  a 
staff  radiographer. 


Haas,  Pauling 
receive  awards 
at  conference 

Phi  Beta  Lambda  (PBL)  members  David  A. 
Haas,  PBL  president  and  computer  science  stu- 
dent from  Williamsport,  and  Paul  H.  Pauling, 
accounting  student  from  Montgomery,  received 
awards  at  the  13th  Annual  Phi  Beta  Lambda 
State  Leadership  Conference. 

The  conference,  held  April  6  to  April  8  at 
the  Marriott  Hotel  in  Harrisburg,  was  an 
academic  competition  among  Pennsylvania 
chapters  of  Phi  Beta  Lambda.  The  testing 
categories  were  business-related. 

According  to  Paul  W.  Goldfeder,  assistant 
professor  of  business  administration,  Haas 
received  a  third  place  trophy  in  the  Data  Pro- 
cessing II  category  and  Pauling  received  a  Penn- 
sylvania Phi  Beta  Lambda  "Who's  Who"  cer- 
tificate. 

"The  certificate  is  given  to  an  individual 
who  displays  outstanding  participation  and  sup- 
port of  his  local  PBL  chapter,"  said  Goldfeder.

Dairy  herd  management 
students  honored 

Courtesy  College  Information  Office 

Four  dairy  herd  management  students  of 
the  College  were  honored  at  a  recognition  ban- 
quet earher  this  month. 

According  to  Robert  A.  Patton,  instructor 
of  dairy  herd  management,  the  honored  students 
received  plaques  at  the  first  of  what  will  be  an 
annual  event. 

Timothy  L.  Beabes,  of  Somerset  RD  2,  was 
honored  for  outstanding  scholarship. 

Pamela  Jo  Crom,  of  Jamestown  RD  1,  was 
recognized  for  her  outstanding  contribution  to 
the  dairy  herd  management  curriculum. 

William  Fleckinger,  of  Ligonier,  was 
recognized  as  a  hardworking  individual. 

Barbara  J.  Bair,  of  Mill  Hall  RD  2,  was 
honored  as  an  outstanding  student  in  dairy  herd 
management. 

The  students  presented  Glenn  Spoerke,  ac- 
ting director  of  the  Earth  Science  Division,  with 
a  "model"  cow  as  a  gesture  of  appreciation. 

Lloyd  Ebersole,  assistant  manager  of  Sire 
Power  Inc.,  of  Tunkhannock,  delivered  a  speech 
on  "Your  Future  in  the  Dairy  Industry"  and 
told  the  students,  "You  must  learn  to  make  op- 
portunities work  for  you." 

Marlin  McClellan,  regional  director  of  the 
State  Department  of  Agriculture  located  in 
Williamsport,  was  cited  by  the  students  for  his 
outstanding  support  of  the  dairy  herd  manage- 
ment program.

â€˘     SPOTLIGHT     â€˘     Monday,  Aprill  6,  1984 

Dental  hygiene  students 
learn  in  many  ways 

Continued  from  Page  11 

Students  enrolled  full-time  for  the  two-year  program  all  work  in  the  clinic  -  which  is  open 
36  hours  a  week.  Not  all  of  the  students  work  in  the  clinic  at  the  same  times,  she  pointed  out. 
second-year  students  work  in  the  clinic  12  hours  per  semester  and  first-year  students  put  in 
hours  per  semester. 

Clinic  hours  were  expanded  recently,  Mrs.  Nestarick  said,  by  adding  an  extra  hour  of 
clinic  time  per  student  and,  if  all  goes  as  planned,  the  clinic  will  be  open  evenings  in  the  Fall 
1984  semester. 

Special  groups  treated  at  the  clinic  include  Hope  Enterprises,  Head  Start,  Lycoming 
County  BLAsT  Intermediate  Unit,  local  nursing  homes,  the  Crippled  Children's  Society,  and 
Alternatives  to  PrisonAVork  Release. 

The  clinic,  Mrs.  Nestarick  said,  welcomes  the  public's  participation  in  the  the  lab  as 
hands-on  experience  is  vital  for  the  students  to  gain  experience  in  dental  hygiene  procedures. 

During  their  fourth  semester,  dental  hygiene  students  go  out  to  various  dental  offices  at 
clinics  and  do  rotational  assignments  at  Allenwood  Prison,  the  Geisinger  Medical  Center  in 
Danville,  local  dental  offices,  and  in  the  Jersey  Shore  Area  School  System. 
Program  fully  accredited 

The  dental  hygiene  program,  which  started  at  the  College  in  1978,  is  fully  accredited  by 
the  American  Dental  Hygiene  Association.  The  program  offers  the  theoretical  and  clinical 
training  needed  for  a  variety  of  dental  hygiene  careers.  The  program  provides  a  diversified 
background  to  prepare  students  for  licensing  exams,  for  additional  education,  and  for  jobs. 
The  general  objective  of  the  program,  however,  is  to  prepare  students  to  successfully  pass  the 
National  Dental  Hygiene  Board  examinations,  the  Northeast  Regional  Boards,  the  required  ex- 
aminations for  selected  states,  and  to  qualify  for  employment  as  dental  hygienists. 

Hygienists  are  employed  by  dentists  in  private  dental  practices,  research,  government 
health  agencies,  school  systems,  hospitals  and  industrial  clinics,  military  servicesd  and  in  dental 
hygiene  education  programs. 

The  College's  official  placement  report  for  graduates  of  August  1981  and  May  1982  show- 
ed excellent  job  placement  for  this  field.  Of  the  graduates  responding  -  13  -  100  percent  were 
employed  in  their  field.   Average  starting  salary  was  $16,400.

Monday,  April  16,  1984     â€˘     SPOTLIGHT     â€˘     21 


Human  Service  Program  Approved 


Courtesy  College  In/ormalion  Office 

The  College  Board  of  Trustees  in  March  ap- 
proved a  new  associate  degree  program  in  the 
human  services  field.  The  two-year  program  is 
scheduled  to  begin  in  Fall  1984. 

The  program.  Human  Service,  would  train 
people  for  entry-level  jobs  or  for  transfer  to  four- 
year  programs.  Graduates  would  be  trained  for 
positions  in  youth  and  aging  programs,  senior 
citizens  centers,  drug  and  alcohol  counseling 
programs,  child  care  development  agencies,  and 
correctional  facilities. 

Statistics  gathered  during  a  feasibility  study 
for  the  program  indicate  there  are  500,000  peo- 
ple now  working  is  such  positions  across  the 
country. 

Survey  taken 

A  College  survey  of  area  agencies  indicated 
that  tight  budget  constraints  will  make  the 
paraprofessional  human  service  worker  an  at- 
tractive alternative  to  the  applicant  whose 
credentials  require  higher  pay  or  to  the  untrained 
applicant. 

The  program,  budgeted  at  a  cost  of  $40,327 
for  16  students  for  the  first  year,  would  require 
hiring  a  lead  instructor,  with  some  courses  being 
taught  by  current  faculty. 

More   than   60  percent   of  the   required 
courses  come  from  those  currently  offered. 
These  include  general  education  courses  in  com- 
munication, math,  science,  physical  education, 
psychology,  sociology,  and  government. 

Available  to  currenlly-employed 

Core  Human  Service  courses  will  be 
available  to  full-time  students,  part-time  students 
currently  working  in  the  field  who  seek  creden- 
tials, and  other  students  who  select  Human  Ser- 
vice courses  as  electives. 

With  courses  scheduled  in  the  late  after- 
noon, evenings  and  on  weekends.  Human  Ser- 
vice will  be  available  to  part-time  students  who 
are  currently  employed. 

Twenty-four  agencies  responding  to  a 
survey  indicated  they  have  65  current  employees 
who  could  benefit  from  all  or  a  portion  of  the 
proposed  program. 

The  new  Human  Service  program  will  train 
students  in  the  general  therapeutic,  supportive, 
and  preventive  skills  needed  to  serve  people  with 
emotional,  developmental,  social  or  physical 
problems.    Students  develop  counseling,  crisis 


intervention,  group  work  and  case  management 
skills.  They  apply  these  general  skills  through 
studying  agencies  and  though  internships  in  the 
field. 

A  graduate  of  the  Human  Service  program 
should  be  able  to:  provide  generic  therapeutic, 
supportive  and  preventive  services  for  people 
with  emotional,  developmental,  social,  or 
physical  problems;  demonstrate  knowledge  of 
social  and  human  service  delivery  systems  and 
their  role  in  the  local  and  national  community; 
identify  and  link  clients  with  resources  and  ser- 
vices provided  by  local  human  service  agencies, 
and  apply  systematic  procedures  to  identify  pro- 
blems. 

Addilional  goals  listed 

Graduates  should  also  be  able  to  provide 
basic  individual  and  group  counseling  techniques 
to  address  identified  problems;  to  serve  as  client 
advocates  within  a  variety  of  agency  settings  and 
facilitate  movements  of  clients  through  social 
service  systems  maintain  progress  and  case  notes 
and  write  objective  and  accurate  reports;  con- 
tribute to  developing  systematic  programs  for 
personal  change;  listen  actively  to  clients,  col- 
leagues, and  the  community;  apply  principles  of 
psychology,  sociology  and  biology  to  human 
issues. 

Finally,  graduates  should  be  able  to  unders- 
tand and  respect  cultural  differences  which  affect 
behavior  and  beliefs;  contribute  to  effective  agen- 
cy planning,  budgeting  and  management,  and 
understand  and  apply  the  interrelationship  of 
physical,  social  and  mental  well-being. 
Job  polenlial  good 

According  to  a  survey  conducted  by  the 
College,  placement  potential  is  good  for  the  new 
program.  Sixty-eight  and  two-tenths  percent  of 
the  agencies  surveyed  indicated  they  would  be  at- 
tracted to  new  employees  with  such  general  train- 
ing. 

Field  placements  will  be  an  important  part 
of  the  program.  Agencies  responding  to  the 
survey  indicated  a  strong  willingness  to 
cooperate  with  the  College  in  providing  both 
short-term  field  training  and  long-term  intern- 
ships. 

Human  Services  will  be  made  available 
through  the  Integrated  Studies  Division  of  The 
Williamsport  Area  Community  College.  More 
information  is  available  by  calling  the  division 
office  at  (717)  326-3761,  Ext.  331.

Former  legislator 

TO  SPEAK 
AT  COMMENCEMENT 

Lawyer  and  former  legislator  Ms.  Sarah  V/eddington  will  be  the  guest 
speaker  at  the  College's  May  12  commencement,  according  to  Lawrence 
W.  Emery  Jr.,  director  of  counseling,  career  development,  and  placement. 

Ms.  Weddington,  director  of  the  Texas  office  of  State-Federal  Rela- 
tions in  Washington,  D.C.,  was  the  first  woman  ever  to  be  elected  to  the 
Texas  House  of  Representatives  where  she  served  for  three  terms. 

Ms.  Weddington  also  was  the  attorney  who  won  the  landmark 
Supreme  Court  decision  which  established  that  U.S.  Constitution 
guarantees  to  women  the  right  to  terminate  a  pregnancy. 

She  has  been  featured  in  publications  such  is  Ms,  Working  Women, 
People,  and  the  Washing/on  Post  and  has  been  a  guest  on  programs  such 
as  "Today"  and  "Good  Morning  America". 


COMMENCEMENT 
REHEARSAL  THURSDAY 

There  will  be  a  commencement  rehearsal  at  3  p.m.  this  Thurs- 
day in  the  Academic  Center  Auditorium,  according  to  Lawrence  W. 
Emery  Jr.,  director  of  counseling,  career  development,  and  place- 
ment. 

Commencement  will  be  held  on  Saturday,  May  12  at  2  p.m. 

On  that  day,  students  who  have  signed  up  to  take  part  in  the 
academic  procession  are  to  meet  in  the  hallway  on  the  second  floor  of 
the  Academic  Center.  Additional  information  is  to  be  given  at  the 
rehearsal. 

Students  who  have  not  yet  signed  up  to  lake  part  may  still  do  so 
by  going  to  Room  157,  Learning  Resources  Center,  Emery  said. 

For  the  May  ceremony,  four  tickets  have  been  sent  to  those 
already  signed  up.  Two  tickets  have  been  sent  to  those  who  petition- 
ed to  graduate.  The  director  said  those  seeking  additional  tickets 
should  stop  at  the  Counseling,  Career  Development,  and  Placement 
Office,  Room  157,  Learning  Resources  Center. 

On  commencement  day,  students  will  proceed  from  the 
Academic  Center  -  following  traditional  order,  after  platform  guests 
and  faculty  -  to  the  Bardo  Gym.  Students  will  be  divided  according 
to  alphabet  and  degree-earned.

SPOTLIGHT     â€˘     Monday,  April  23,  1984 

Long  range  planning  works 
throughout  vacation  to  create 
an  efficient  and  effective  College 

...In  the  SPOTLIGHT'S  opinion 

The  College's  long  range  planning  process,  involving  various  members  of  the  College 
staff,  administration,  and  community  members,  recently  hosted  a  three-day  visit  for  the  Mid- 
dle Stales  Association  evaluators. 

The  purpose  of  the  evaluators'  visit  was  to  reaffirm  the  College's  accreditation. 

According  to  a  lew  of  the  long  range  planning  personnel,  the  evaluators  were  "impress- 
ed" with  the  College  and  "overwhelmed"  at  the  quality  of  the  education,  student  reactions, 
and  attitudes  of  the  College  staff  and  faculty. 

Perhaps  many  of  us  lake  advantage  of  what  is  developing  around  us  at  the  College. 
These  long  range  planning  worl<ers  invest  many  long  hours  of  hard  worl<  to  devise  pam- 
phlets and  planning  manuals  and  to  travel  for  seminars  to  benefit  the  College's  future. 

A  planning  manual  titled,  "A  Strategic  Planning  Model",  summarizes  long  range  plann- 
ing accurately:  "In  reality,  the  planning  process  never  ends  as  it  is  cyclical  in  nature  and 
results  in  a  short-range  plan  of  operation  being  generated  annually." 

While  students  and  faculty  break  for  the  summer,  the  long  range  planning  process 
works  diligently  throughout  vacations  In  an  effort  to  create  a  modern,  more  efficient  and  ef- 
fective institution. 


Graduation  jitters:   Will 
you  be  there  May  12? 

...In  the  SPOTLIGHT'S  opinion 

What  am  I  going  to  do?  Only  seven  days  of  school  left  and  I  have  two  finals,  a  test,  an 
English  composition  paper,  and  an  advertising  project  to  do.  I  have  all  this  work  to  do  -  let 
alone  get  ready  lor  graduation. 

Every  night  this  week  I  have  to  do  homework  in  order  to  get  everything  finished.  When 
will  I  have  time  (o  go  to  all  the  end-of-the-year  and  graduation  parties?  I  almost  forgot  to  get 
my  graduation  apparel.  Oh  no,  how  much  will  that  cost  me?  What  do  I  need?  A  cap,  gown, 
hood,  tassel''  If  I  make  it  through  graduation  alive,  it  will  be  a  miracle! 
Who  to  Invite 

Everyone  always  asks:  are  you  nervous  about  graduation?  Well,  to  put  it  mildly,  I'm  a 
long  way  past  nervous  -  leaning  toward  scared. 

All  those  people  will  be  there  watching  me  -  faculty,  staff,  administrators,  family,  and 
friends.   With  my  luck,  I'll  probably  trip  over  rriy  gown  when  I  go  up  to  get  my  diploma. 

Let's  see,  I  have  four  tickets  to  graduation.  Who  will  I  invite?  I  have  so  many  relatives  to 
invite  -  mom,  dad,  my  sister  Martha,  brother  Sam,  Uncle  Vester,  Aunt  Phyllis,  and  Grandma 
Gertrude. 

The  goldfish  too? 

II  Uncle  Vester  comes,  he'll  bring  Aunt  Phyllis'  goldfish  because  she  won't  want  to  leave 
them  unattended    I  guess  those  two  are  out. 

I  got  it!  Sister  Martha  can  sit  on  Grandma  Gertrude's  lap  and  then  they'll  only  count  as 
one  person.   Boy,  aren't  I  a  genius? 

Well,  enough  ol  this  nightmare.  Off  to  the  refrigerator  for  a  Coke  and  some  potato  chips 
and  onto  the  homework  or  I  won't  need  the  cap  and  gown  because  I  won't  be  graduating 
anywayi 


Monday,  April  23,  1984     â€˘     SPOTLIGHT     < 

LETTERS 

(The  following  letter  concerns  a  Whaddya'  say  question  In  the  March  12  SPOTLIGHT.    II 
read:  "Does  a  pregnancy  out-of-wedlock  have  anything  to  do  with  being  qualified  for  the  Na- 
tional Honor  Society^") 
To  SPOTLIGHT  readers: 

Is  this  supposed  to  be  an  example  of  journalism?  I  call  it  an  example  of  garbage  in  print. 
There  are  four  qualifications  for  the  National  Honor  Society:  1 )  scholarship.  2)  leadership, 
3)  character,  4)  service.  Your  feature  makes  no  attempt  to  explain  this.  All  you've  done  is 
to  quote  half  a  dozen  uninformed  people,  and  you've  given  the  impression  that  the  correct 
answer  to  the  question  is  "No"  because  six  out  of  seven  people  said  that,  and  the  majority 
shoujd  be  right. 

Your  poll,  like  many  others,  amounts  to  the  polling  of  ignorance.  Journalism  is  the 
reporting  of  facts,  vi/hich  may  appropriately  be  analyzed  in  editorials,  but  pages  like  this  one 
are  a  disgrace  to  the  profession.  Pregnancy  out-of-wedlock  has  a  great  deal  to  do  with  both 
character  and  leadership.   Hope  you'll  present  the  facts  in  a  coming  issue. 

Wanda  R.  Shirk 
Publications  Adviser,  Northern  Potter  High  School 


Editor's  Note: 

The  question  did  not,  as  you  stated,  make  any  attempt  to  explain  the  qualifications  in  the 
society  (NHS).  We  do  not  know  that  the  students  who  answered  the  question  were  totally 
uninformed. 

We  do  not  present  the  Whaddya'  say  feature  as  a  scientific  poll,  but  rather  simply  as  a 
sampling  of  opinions  of  students  around  the  College  in  the  hope  of  generating  discussion. 
Students  are  indiscriminately  selected  at  random.  As  with  any  "person  in  the  street"  poll,  the 
newspaper  does  not  offer  the  responses  as  "gospel".  The  students'  answers  are  reported 
as  they  give  them 

Since  the  question,  when  asked,  did  not  provide  the  background  of  the  national  news 
story  upon  it  was  based  or  upon  information  about  NHS  and  since  we  presumed  the  general 
readership  was  informed,  we  now  present  the  following  information  concerning  the  society: 

There  are  four  criteria  which  one  has  to  meet:  scholarship,  leadership,  service,  and 
character.  Students  must  have  all  the  qualities  to  be  admitted  into  the  society 

The  scholarship  requirements  call  for  students  to  have  a  cumulative  scholastic  average 
of  85  percent  or  a  B,  which  is  3  0  on  a  4.0  scale,  or  the  equivalent  standard  of  excellence. 
This  is  the  minimum  cumulative  scholastic  average,  but  faculty  councils  in  individual  schools 
may  raise  this  qualification  requirement.  These  councils  may  also  require  a  certain  number 
of  prerequisite  academic  courses. 

Qualified  students  are  selected  by  a  majority  vote  of  the  faculty  council  on  the  preceding 
criteria.  These  criteria  all  have  a  working  definition.  According  to  the  National  Honor  Society 
handbook,  the  student  who  exercises  leadership  is  resourceful  in  proposing  new  problems, 
applying  principles  and  making  suggestions,  demonstrates  leadership  in  promoting  school 
activities,  exercises  influence  on  peers  in  upholding  school's  ideals,  contributes  ideas  that 
improve  the  civic  life  of  the  school,  is  able  to  delegate  responsibilities,  and  many  others. 

Concerning  the  service  criteria,  the  student  who  serves  is  willing  to  uphold  scholarship 
and  maintain  a  loyal  school  attitude,  participates  in  some  outside  activity  such  as  Girl  Scouts, 
Boy  Scouts,  church  groups,  volunteer  services  for  the  aged,  poor  or  disadvantaged,  and 
family  duties,  volunteers  dependable  and  well-organized  assistance,  and  is  willing  to 
sacrifice  to  offer  assistance,  cheerfully  and  enthusiastically  renders  any  requested  service  to 
the  school,  and  is  willing  to  represent  the  class  or  school  in  interclass  and  interscholastic 
competition. 

To  meet  the  requirement  of  character,  the  student  must  take  criticism  willingly  and  ac- 
cept recommendations  graciously,  constantly  exemplify  desireable  qualities  of  personality, 
cooperate  by  complying  with  school  regulations  concerning  property,  programs,  office, 
IPLEASE  CONTINUE  READING  ON  NEXT  PAGE| 


6     .     SPOTLIGHT    â€˘     Monday,  April  23, 1984 

National  Honor  Society 

[CONTINUED  FROM  PRECEDING  PAGEl 

demonstrate  the  highest  standards  of  honesty  and  reliability,  show  courtesy,  concern  and 
respect  for  others,  observe  instructions  and  ruies,  punctuality,  and  faithfulness  both  inside 
and  outside  the  classroom,  and  a  coupie  of  other  definitions. 

We  feel  faculty  councils'  selection  criteria  and  procedures  should  be  fair  and  objective. 
According  to  ivan  B.  Gluckman,  National  Association  of  Secondary  School  Principals 
legal  counsel,  "pregnancy,  vi/hether  vi^ithin  or  without  of  wedlock,  cannot  be  the  basis  for 
automatic  rejection  under  judicial  interpretations  of  federal  law.  But  pregnancy  can  be  con- 
sidered as  one  determinant  of  character,  as  defined  by  a  particular  faculty  council.  Pregnan- 
cy can  be  so  considered  only  if  evidence  of  paternity  is  similarly  regarded  as  indicative  of 
character." 

Zinsser's  On  Writing  Well 
prepares  students  for  college 

William  Zinsser's  book.  On  Writing  Well,  is  not  your  piwiwÂ»<' >S!aaM^Â«i^ 
ordinary  textbook,  but  rather  an  informal  guide  to  writing 
non-fiction.  On  Writing  Well'  grew  out  of  a  course  that 
Zinsser  taught  at  Yale  University,  Zinsser  makes  learning 
fun  and  easy,  in  this  second  edition.  Zinsser  tries  to 
answer  the  questions  that  were  asked  most  often  by 
writers,  editors,  teachers,  educators,  students,  and 
business  people. 

Although  this  book  is  about  writing,  it  is  not  meant  just  for  "writers".  Its  points  are  valid 
for  all  people  who  have  to  do  writing  just  to  get  along  in  their  jobs  or  at  school. 

In  this  edition,  he  has  included  a  chapter  entitled  "Writing  in  Your  Job"  in  which  he  gives 
tips  on  the  interoffice  memo  and  the  marketing  analysis,  as  forms  of  writing,  using  them  as 
examples  of  the  ability  or  inability  of  an  employee  to  state  an  idea  or  a  set  of  facts  clearly  and 
concisely- 
Enlarged  upon  in  this  book  is  the  chapter  on  sports  writing  in  which  he  tackles  such 
troublesome  new  problems  as  jargon,  sexism,  and  impersonality.  He  has  included  a  new 
section  on  the  lineage  and  techniques  of  American  humor. 

On  Writing  Well  is  illustrated  with  brief  passages  from  many  of  Zinsser's  favorite  non- 
fiction  writers  such  as  George  Orwell  and  John  (vIcPhee, 

On  Writing  Well  is  instructive  but  never  boring,  proving  the  book's  central  point:  That 
there  is  no  subject  which  cannot  be  made  accessible  if  the  writer  goes  about  his  work  with 
humanity  and  cares  enough  to  write  well. 

This  easy-reading,  hard-cover  book  (Harper  &  Row  Publisher)  is  an  $1 1 ,95  sound  In- 
vestment for  anyone  who  has  to  write  either  on  the  job  or  at  school.  It's  recommended  that 
this  book  be  read  over  the  long  summer  months  to  get  prepared  for  next  semester  or  that 
new  job.

College  vacations,  by  journalism  and  other  Interested  students  of  The  Wllllamsport  Area 
Community  College

Harmony  Theatre 
presents  'GodspelP 
tomorrow  evening 

"Godspell"  will  be  presented  by  the  Har- 
mony Theatre  at  8  p.m.,  tomorrow  in  the 
Academic  Center  Auditorium,  according  to  Mrs. 
JoAnn  R.  Fremiotti,  coordinator  of  intramural 
athletics  and  College  activities. 

"Godspell",  as  conceived  by  John-Michael 
Tebelak,  is  a  jubilant  celebration  of  the  Gospel 
as  told  by  St.  Matthew,  she  said. 

Many  theatrical  devices  will  be  used.  They 
include  pantomime,  children's  games,  charades, 
puppetry,  vaudeville  antics,  the  question-answer 
technique  of  minstrel  shows,  tap  and  soft  shoe, 
dancing,  pop,  rock,  light  musical  comedy,  and 
drama,  according  to  Lawrence  Carra,  professor 
of  play  directing,  Carnegie-Melon  University. 

Appearing  in  the  cast  are  Chris  Hendricks, 
of  Williamsport  as  Jesus  and  Joel  Gori,  of 
Lewisburg  as  John  the  Baptist/Judas. 

Also  appearing  are  Mary  Jean  Casale  (co- 
producer  of  Harmony  Theatre),  Bemadette 
Haas,  Sherry  Hillyard  (choreographer);  Paula 
M.  Patchin,  I^rry  Savitsky,  and  Kalyn  Shaible, 
all  of  Williamsport;  Cindy  Ross,  of  Sunbury; 
and  Eric  Shaefer,  Ralston. 

This  event  is  sponsored  by  the  College's 
Special  Events  Committee.  It  is  free  to  all 
students  with  a  validated  student  ID. 

SGA  appoints 
new  officers 

New  officers  have  been  appointed  for  the 
Student  Government  Association  (SGA)  for  the 
1984-85  school  year,  according  to  Miss  Ruth  A. 
Fischer,  general  studies  student  from 
Williamsport  and  current  SGA  president. 

Miss  Fischer  will  be  graduated  this  Spring 
and  the  current  vice  president,  Steven  D.  Met- 
zker,  business  management  student  from 
Williamsport,  will  fill  the  position  of  president. 

Greg  S.  Stewart,  computer  science  student 
from  Linden,  will  hold  the  positin  of  vice  presi- 
dent, said  Miss  Fischer. 

Miss  Fischer  added  that  next  year  the  SGA 
Office  will  be  located  in  Room  A138,  Center  for 
Lifelong  Education.

Summer  brochures 
now  available 

Brochures  for  summer  non-credit  courses 
now  are  available,  according  to  Mrs.  Carol  F. 
Kaufman,  coordinator  of  campus-based  ac- 
tivities. 

Three  new  summer  courses  have  been  add- 
ed. These  are  "Programming  Your  TI"  (Texas 
Instruments  calculator),  which  will  be  taught  by 
Anthony  Davies;  "Colonial  Lampshades", 
which  will  be  taught  by  Craftswoman  Bobbi 
Dwyer;  and  a  course  which  will  be  taught  by 
WILQ  sports  director  Bill  Keen  called  "Arm- 
chair Football".  This  course  deals  with  obtain- 
ing a  spectator  knowledge  of  football,  according 
to  Mrs.  Kaufman. 

"Since  we've  developed  the  computerized 
mailing  list  over  the  last  two  years,  we've  mailed 
in  the  neighborhood  of  11,000  brochures  based 
on  the  mail  list  generated  by  the  administrative 
computer,"  Mrs.  Kaufman  noted. 

Mrs.  Kaufman  also  noted  that  for  the  first 
time,  community-based  activities  are  listed  in  the 
summer  brochure.

Intensive,  special 

five-day  course 

will  be  held  May  7  to  11 

"Kubvan  100  General  Repair"  is  an  inten- 
sive five-day  course  which  will  be  held  at  the  Col- 
lege May  7  to  1 1 ,  according  to  Grant  L.  Martin, 
coordinator  of  specialized  technical  educational 
programs. 

Grumman  Allied  will  select  10  trainees  from 
different  parts  of  the  Northeast  to  take  part  in 
the  course,  according  to  Martin, 

"This  course  involves  instruction  in  general 
repair  and  maintenance  jobs  that  most  frequent- 
ly occur  in  the  workshop,"  said  Martin. 

The  course  is  being  developed  by  William 
Debolt,  director  of  the  Technology  Division. 
Two  faculty  members,  Rodman  H.  Perry, 
automotive  instructor,  and  Lawrence  P.  Stabler, 
assistant  professor  of  automotive  technology, 
will  be  instructing  the  course,  according  to  Mar- 


College  to  host  seminar; 
acquaints  store  personnel 
with  computer  processes 

The  Middle  Atlantic  College  Stores  Com- 
puter Seminar  will  be  held  at  the  College  on 
Tuesday  and  Wednesday,  May  22  and  23,  accor- 
ding to  Ms.  Sandra  L.  Rosenberger,  coordinator 
of  community-based  activities. 

The  seminar  will  be  sponsored  by  the 
Center  for  Lifelong  Education  and  the  College 
bookstore.  It  will  include  two  different  classes  to 
instruct  college  store  personnel  in  computer  pro- 
gramming, according  to  Ms.  Rosenberger. 

Alex  W.  Bailey,  professor  of  business  ad- 
ministration, will  teach  a  class  in  word  process- 
ing and  Thomas  M.  Winder,  assistant  professor 
of  computer  science,  will  teach  a  class  in  basic 
computer  programming. 

"We  expect  35  people  from  all  over  the 
Mid-Atlantic  area  (Delaware,  Maryland,  New 
Jersey,  West  Virginia,  and  Washington,  D.C)  to 
attend  this  two-day  conference,"  said  Ms. 
Rosenberger.

lit  course 

Writers  and  others  in  the  "book  world" 
were  featured  during  the  spring  semester  offering 
for  literature  lovers,  EDU  121,  Children's  and 
Young  Adult  Literature.  The  course  was  in- 
itiated by  Mrs.  Mary  Jane  West,  associate  pro- 
fessor of  English  and  economics. 

Among  speakers  were: 

Mrs.  Betsy  Rider,  who,  with  her  husband, 
John  Rider,  own  Otto's  Book  Store.  Mrs.  Rider 
spoke  on  "sexism"  and  other  "isms"  in 
children's  books. 

Dr.  M.  Louise  Stabler,  owner  and  operator 
of  Stabler's  Mason  Reading  CUnic,  spoke  on 
"Getting  People  from  Seven-to-Seventy  to 
Read". 

Mrs.  Susan  Middleton,  an  executive  with 
Headstart  and  wife  of  Dr.  James  E.  Middleton, 
director  of  Integrated  Studies  Division,  dealt 
with  "How  to  Get  Toddler's  Ready  to  Read". 

Mrs.  Kathryn  Lumley,  member  of  the  Col- 
lege Board  of  Trustees,  organized  the  Reading  Is 
Fun  program  better  known  as  RIF.  She  is  also 
the  author  of  13  children's  instructional  books. 
Some  were  illustrated  by  Charles  Shultz.  Her 
topic  was  "How  to  Become  a  Writer  of  Chilren's 
Books". 

"The  course  is  offered  every  other  spring," 
said  Mrs.  West,  adding  that  "if  enough  interest 
is  generated,  the  class  could  be  offered  more 
often.'

Members  of  SME 
to  attend  dinner, 
technical  session 

Members  of  the  Student  Society  of 
Manufacturing  and  Engineering  Club  (SME)  will 
attend  a  dinner  and  technical  session  Monday, 
May  14,  at  the  Sheraton  Motor  Inn, 
Williamsport,  according  to  Chalmer  C.  Van 
Horn,  club  adviser. 

Dinner  will  be  served  at  6;30  p.m.  Reserva- 
tions should  be  made  by  next  Monday,  April  30, 
and  must  be  paid  in  advance. 

Students  interested  in  attending  the  dinner 
and  technical  session  should  contact  Van  Horn 
in  Room  107,  Administration  Building. 

The  technical  session  will  begin  at  8  p.m. 
and  anyone  is  welcome  to  attend,  said  Van 
Horn. 

Thomas  Reiley,  manager  of  the  Harrisburg 
District  of  Schrader  Bellows  Division  of  the 
Scovill  Fluid  Power  Group,  assisted  by  Burt 
Martinez,  national  sales  manager  for  robotics, 
will  demonstrate  a  Pick  &  Place  Motion  Mate  # 
1  Robot,  explaining  its  controls  and  capabilities. 

Schrader  Bellows  is  the  leading  American 
manufacturer  of  the  full  line  of  industrial  robots. 
Videotapes  of  the  various  types  will  also  be 
discussed,  stated  Van  Horn. 

Technical  sessions  are  held  every  month  ex- 
cept for  the  summer  months,  he  noted. 


Students  complete  project 
in  Susquehanna  Township 


Courtesy  College  Informalion  Office 
One  project  completed  last  month  by  Col- 
lege students  saved  residents  of  Susquehanna 
Township  (Nisbet  area)  about  $50,000,  accor- 
ding to  Arthur  L.  Shuman,  roadmaster  and 
maintenance  superintendent  of  Susquehanna 
Township. 

Postsecondary  students  in  construction 
carpentry  and  carpentry  and  building  construc- 
tion technology  as  well  as  secondary  masonry 
students  built  a  48  by  60  foot  cinder  block 
building  for  the  township.  Final  estimated  cost 
to  the  township  was  $20,000. 

Students  did  about  85  percent  of  the  work, 
doing  nearly  everything  except  the  plumbing  and 
wiring. 


The  16-foot  high  structure  is  being  used  for 
offices  and  maintenance  space  by  the  township. 
Only  the  exterior  of  the  building  remains  to  be 
finished  and  the  township  has  contracted  to  have 
that  done. 

Ralph  Home,  director  of  the  Construction 
Technology  Division,  said  the  students  in  his 
division  have  been  involved  in  about  a  half 
dozen  projects  in  the  past  year,  including  work 
at  St.  Anthony's  Center,  Williamsport,  and  the 
Boy  Scout  headquarters,  Northway  Road, 
Williamsport. 

Students  from  construction  carpentry, 
carpentry  and  building  construction  technology, 
plumbing  and  geating,  and  electrical  technology 
have  all  worked  on  the  projects.

Rebuilding  Piper  Comanche  engine 
maior  project  for  aviation  students 

*****J  r        J  Gleams  with  gol( 


By  Kalhy  M.  Gilbert 
Of  The  SPOTLrGHT  Staff 

Second-year  students  at  the  Aviation  Campus 
at  the  Williamsport-Lycoming  Airport  m  Mon- 
toursville  have  been  working  on  a  major  project: 
rebuilding  the  engine  of  a  1958  Piper  Comanche 
airplane.  ,  ,    â€ž. 

The  airplane  is  number  78  off  the  Piper  Air- 
craft Corporlalion  production  line  in  Lock  Haven. 
The  students  removed  the  180  Avco-Lycoming 
engine  from  the  airplane  by  using  a  hoist.  The 
engine  was  then  taken  to  the  shop  and  placed  on  an 
engine  stand  where  the  students  disassembled  it. 

During  the  disassembly,  the  students  did  a 
dimensional  inspection  with  a  micrometer.  The 
magnallux  machine  was  used  to  detect  cracks  in  the 
metal  and  the  zyglo  machine  was  also  used  to  detect 
cracks  in  non-ferrous  metals. 

Other  parts  checked 
Other  engine  parts  were  checked  and  replaced 
if  they  showed  any  wear.  The  cylinders  were  sent  to 
Penn  Yan  Aero  Service,  Penn  Yan,  N.Y.  to  have 
work  done  on  them. 

The  owner  of  the  airplane.  Herb  Wahl,  of 
Herb's  Model  Motors  of  Forksville,  is  a  builder  of 
small  aircraft  engines  for  model  airplanes.  Wahl 
stated,  that  he  started  building  aircraft  engines  in 
1966.  At  that  time,  he  began,  on  a  small  scale, 
making  engine  parts  and  taking  orders  for  them. 

Ten  years  ago,  he  got  permission  from  Bill 
Brown  to  reproduce  Brown's  classic,  Brown  Jr., 
engine.  Wahl  said  this  engine  was  called  the  "An- 
niversary Brown"  to  honor  the  first  official  gas 
engine  for  model  airplanes.  This  engine  was  in- 
vented shortly  before  Brown  moved  to  Stale  College 
to  study  engineering  at  Penn  State  University. 


Gleams  with  gold 

Another  model  engine  produced  by  Wahl  is  the 
Ohisson  Commemorative  Engine,  gleaming  with 
24-karet  gold  plating.  It  is  named  after  the  original 
1938  Ohisson  Gold  Seal  engine,  Wahl  said,  adding 
that  his  version  includes  one  or  two  special  touches 
like  a  gold  cylinder  to  distinguish  it  from  the 
original  engine. 

Irwin  Ohisson,  inventor  of  the  Ohissoii  Gold 
Seal  engine  insisted  on  signing  all  900  cards  himself, 
stated  Wahl.  Ohisson  is  one  of  the  pioneers  in  the 
field,  coming  after  the  king,  "Bill  Brown". 

Wahl  explained  that  he  works  strictly  on  a 
limited  edition  basis  with  each  engine  model,  cut- 
ting off  production  after  a  certain  number  of  orders 
has  been  received.  The  orders  come  in  response  to 
small  ads  placed  in  collectors'  journals  and  notices 
to  hobbyists  on  a  special  mailing  list. 
Incentive  provided 

"Once  you  have  accepted  advance  payments 
on  900  engines,  that  gives  you  the  incentive  to  get 
out  in  the  shop  to  produce,"  Wahl  stated.  Each 
engine  is  packed  in  a  gold-colored  shipping  box 
with  a  numbered  certificate  of  endorsement  and 
authenticity  card  signed  by  the  inventor,  Irwin 
Ohisson,  of  Los  Angeles. 

Wahl  met  James  E.  Doebler,  instructor  of 
aviation,  a  few  years  before  he  began  work  at  the 
College.  Doebler  had  been  doing  the  maintenance 
on  the  aircraft  and  was  familiar  with  it,  Wahl  ex- 
plained. 

Wahl  said  he  came  to  the  Aviation  Campus  to 
ask  Doebler  who  he  would  recommend  to  do  the 
maintenance  on  his  airplane.
