Monday,  April  23,  1984     â€˘     SPOTLIGHT     â€˘     11 


THEY'RE  Young... 
THEY'RE  'Older... 
And  THEY'RE  Meeting 
IN  THE  Classrooms 


A  SPOTLIGHT  Special  Report 
By  Rodney  D.  Hill 


12     â€˘     SPOTLIGHT     â€˘     Monday.  April  23,  1984 


More  and  more  so-called  "older"  students  -- 
from  mid-20s  and  on  up  --  are  returning  to  the 
College  classrooms... 

How  do  they  and  the  so-called  "younger" 
students  ~  just  out  of  high  school  --  feel  about 
being  in  the  same  learning  situation? 


The  mix  of  "older"  and  "younger"  students  at  the 
College  undoubtedly  presents  a  rather  new  experience  for 
most  who  enter  the  College  as  students. 

"Older"  students  lend  to  believe  -â–   if  casual  conversa- 
tion can  be  accepted  -  that  they  will  not  be  as  "sharp"  as 
they  once  were.  With  that  in  mind,  they  think  they  will  be 
less  competitive  than  their  classmates  who  are  fresh  out  of 
high  school. 

Younger  students,  on  the  other  hand,  have  just 
cotne  from  a  high  school  environment  and  are  only  familiar 
with  fellow  students  who  are  of  their  own  age  group. 

In  keeping  with  these  thoughts,  three  selected  groups 
were  interviewed: 

--  Older  students  who  are  24  years  of  age  and  older. 

--  Younger  students  18  to  23  years  old. 

--  Faculty  members  selected  indiscrimately  at  random. 


Considering  the  responses,  it  appears  there  is  no  gap 
between  younger  and  older  students. 

The  fact  that  older  students  indicate  they  want  more 
out  of  their  education  seems  rather  common  --  and  seems  to 
be  due  to  their  prior  life-experiences. 

If  the  older  students  were  to  go  back  a  few  years  and 
look  at  themselves,  according  to  other  conversations  during 
the  interviews,  they  would  probably  see  that  the  younger 
students  have  what  could  be  called  a  typical  approach  to  col- 
lege. 

The  older  students,  overall,  seem  to  be  amazed  at 
what  information  they  retained  from  schooling  and  at  the 
progress  they  now  are  making.

Comments 

BY 

Faculty 


Question;  Besides  age,  do  yon  see  any  other  differences  between  the  older  and  younger 
students? 

James  E.  LOGUE,  associate  professor  of  English:  Yes.  For  the  most  pari,  the  older  stu- 
dent seems  to  be  more  motivated  to  do  the  work  of  a  college  student  -  which  is  probably  due 
to  their  experience. 

Ronald  L.  Rock,  professor  of  business  administration:  Generally,  the  older  students 
have  their  goals  established  and  they  know  what  they  are  seeking. 

Richard  Sahn,  instructor  of  sociology  and  psychology:  The  older  student  seems  to  be 
more  interested  in  the  subject  matter  of  the  courses  and  not  just  concerned  about  the  credits 
and  grades. 

Jackie  E.  Welliver,  instructor  of  industrial  drafting:  Yes.  The  older  students  know 
what  they  are  going  for  and,  unlike  the  younger  students,  they  know  what  to  expect. 

Fred  C.  Schaefer,  assistant  professor  of  graphic  arts:  Yes.  The  older  student  seems 
more  serious  about  school  -  especially  the  veterans. 

William  G.  Rummings,  instructor  of  building  construction:   Yes:  Prior  experience. 

Thomas  C.  Shoff,  counselor:  Attitude.  The  older  student  has  life  experience  and  bet- 
ter judgement.  They  know  why  they're  here. 

G.  Robert  Kissbll,  professor  of  history  and  government:  No,  I  don't  see  any  dif- 
ferences other  than  the  maturity  that  older  students  bring  into  the  scene. 

Patrick  D.  Murphy,  assistant  professor  of  advertising  art:  Yes,  there  is  a  definite  dif- 
ference in  the  experiences  of  the  older  and  younger  students.

Question:  Do  you  IhlDk  both  groups  of  sludenls'gel  treated  the  same? 

James  E.  LOGUE:    Yes,  I  Ihink  so. 

Ronald  L.  Rock:    Yes. 

Richard  Sahn:  Yes,  but  I  think  the  students  that  I  have  had  just  out  of  high  school 
tend  to  treat  the  older  students  differently.  I  think  they  are  kind  ofleary  of  the  older  students 
because  the  older  students  are  a  little  like  their  parents  and  instructors  themselves. 

Jackie  E.  Welliver:  Yes.  I  try  to  treat  them  the  same  although  sometimes  it  might 
seem  as  though  I  treat  the  older  students  differently  because  they  are  closer  to  my  age  and  can 
relate  to  a  period  of  time  with  me. 

Fred  C.  SCHAEFER:    Speaking  for  myself,  I  don't  see  any  difference  in  the  treatment. 

William  G.  Rummings:    Yes

same  and  as  much  as  they  try,  never-the-less,  when  you  see  a  person  with  a  more  mature  at- 
titude who  is  willing  to  do  the  work  that  you  are  telling  them  to  do...  then  that  person  will 
probably  get  more  attention. 

G.  Robert  Kissell:     Yes.  Otherwise,  I'd  have  to  fish  for  an  answer. 

Patrick  D.  Murphy:  /  think  they  all  get  treated  the  same  as  far  as  general  lecture.  I 
think  there  is  a  different  level  of  communication  on  individual  bases.

to  their  age  group  --  they  bring  more  experiences  into  the  course. 

Jackie  E.  Welliver:  /  like  teaching  all  students,  but  older  stuents  seem  to  want  more 
information  than  the  younger  students. 

William  G.  Rummings:  /  like  working  with  all  students. 

Thomas  C.  Shoff:  There  is  more  interaction  with  the  younger  and  older  students  and, 
therefore,  it  makes  a  more  interesting  class. 

G.  Robert  Kissell:  /  enjoy  the  older  students  because  they  bring  a  new  insight  into  the 
classroom  and  they  are  motivated. 

Patrick  D.  Murphy:  /  enjoy  older  students.  Maybe  it's  because  there  is  more  com- 
monality.

College  of  the  future... 

This  photograph  of  a  plan  for  the  future  was  taken  by  Thomas  H.  Long,  of  The  SPOTLIGHT 
staff.  Ill  conjunction  with  his  interview  with  Dr.  Robert  L.  Breiider,  College  president  which 
appears  on  the  next  page.  ' 

COMPLETED  CAMPUS  PLAN  key  is  as  follows:  Building  1,  the  Academic  Cenler:  2 
Lifelong  Education  Cenler;  3,  Learning  Resources  Cenler;  4,  Building  Trades  Center-  5 
Gymnasrum;  6,  Technical  Trades  Center;  7,  Automotive  Trades  Center;  13,  Advanced 
Technology  &  Allied  Health  Center;  14,  Professional  Development  Center;  15,  Diesel 
Mechanics  Center;  16,  Student  Center;  17,  Fine  Arts  Center;  18,  College  Service  Center, 
and  19,  Metal  Trades  Center. 

The  light  grey  structures  are  existing  structures.  The  dark  grey  structures  are  new  struc- 
tures. Pedestrian  walkways,  roadways,  and  landscaping  areas  are  self-explainatory 

The  completed  plan  was  drawn-up  by  Hayes,  Large,  Suckling,  Fruth  &  Wedge  Ar- 


Monday,  April  23,  1984     â€˘     SPOTLIGHT 


Major  College  expansion 
will  happen  in  next  decade 

By  Thomas  H.  Long,  Of  The  SPOTLIGHT  Staff 

Over  the  course  of  the  next  10  years,  the  College  is  slated  to  undergo  many  physical 
changes  as  well  as  academic  advancements  in  order  to  keep  it  competitive  in  today's  world  of 
work. 

According  to  Dr.  Robert  L.  Breuder,  College  president,  a  master  plan,  with  a  projected 
cost  of  approximately  $36.5  million,  was  designed  and  drawn  up  by  the  College  architectural 
firm  of  Hayes,  Large,  Suckling,  Fruth,  and  Wedge.  This  plan  was  presented  at  the  April  Col- 
lege Board  of  Trustees  meeting. 

Among  those  things  to  be  updated  in  the  near  future  is  the  Academic  Center  'where  a  com- 
pletely new  electrical  system  is  to  be  installed. 

In  addition,  the  completion  of  the  closing-in  of  the  Technical  Trades  buildings  is  planned. 

Dr.  Breuder  said  with  the  new  space  in  these  buildings  will  house  the  programmable  logic 
curriculum,  secondary  and  faculty  offices  as  well  as  classrooms,  storage  space,  and  other  cur- 
riculum facilities. 

Demololion  this  summer 

Considerations  for  the  distant  future  include  the  possibility  of  a  fine  arts  center  to  be 
located  where  the  administration  building  is  now  standing.  That  building  will  be  demolished 
this  summer,  Dr.  Breuder  said.  All  administration  will  be  moved  into  the  new  Lifelong  Educa- 
tion Center. 

The  Advanced  Technology  and  Health  Services  building  will  be  started  early  next  year, 
with  a  scheduled  completion  time  of  summer  1987.  This  new  structure  will  house  many  pro- 
grams that  now  are  limited  for  space  in  the  Academic  Center  as  well  as  several  new  programs. 
It  is  expected  to  accomodate  about  500  more  students  than  the  College  has  space  for  now,  add- 
ed Dr.  Breuder. 

Street  changes  planned 

Low  on  the  list  of  priorities,  he  said,  for  the  Stage  3  building  program,  but  still  scheduled, 
is  the  construction  of  a  football  field,  a  baseball  diamond,  about  five  tennis  courts,  and  possi- 
ble a  basketball  court,  to  be  located  behind  the  proposed  site  of  new  diesel  facility,  near  the 
Automotive  Trades  Center. 

Overall  plans  for  campus  changes  include  the  "closing  in"  of  Susquehanna  Street  in 

order  to  make  it  a  pedestrian  walkway,  said  Dr.  Breuder.  Another  street  will  be  put  in,  beyond 

the  campus,  on  the  west  side.   Eventually,  a  street  running  east-west  will  connect  Park  Street 

and  the  new  road.   It  will  be  located  beyond  the  railroad  tracks  at  the  south  end  of  campus. 

'Green  areas'  established 

A  "green  area"  will  be  added  near  the  site  of  the  Health  Services  building  and  will  be 
complete  with  walkways.  Located  to  the  east  of  the  green  area  will,  hopefully,  be  a  new 
physical  plant  and  security  building,  incorporated  into  one  unit,  according  to  Thomas  C. 
Large,  architect. 

The  campus,  in  its  completed  phase,  is  projected  to  be  finished  within  the  next  lO-year 
period.  The  figures  for  the  advancements  are  at  today's  prices.  However,  for  every  year  this 
plan  is  delayed,  it  will  cost  an  additional  eight  percent,  to  be  added  to  the  $36.5  million.  Dr. 
Breuder  stated. 

He  added  that  there  will  still  be  a  matter  of  architects'  fees  as  well  as  other  expenses  that 
cannot  yet  be  predicted,  but  he  said  he  is  positive  about  the  future  of  the  College,  "as  a  viable 
and  competitive  institution". 


24     â€˘     SPOTLIGHT     â€˘     Monday,  April  23,  1984 

Involvement,  concern:   key  words 
in  life  of  award-winning  professor 

By  Gregory  W.  Huff 

Of  The  SPOTLIGHT  Staff 

Editor's  Note: 

Ms.  Veronica  M.  Muzic  is  a  professor  of  English  at  the  College  and  the  first  recipient  of 
the  Master  Teacher  Award  introduced  at  the  College  in  1982. 

A  graduate  of  College  Misericordia,  Ms.  Muzic  received  a  master's  degree  in  English 
from  Bucknell  University  and  has  done  postgraduate  work  at  several  universities  including 
the  University  of  New  Hampshire,  Purdue  University,  Texas  AiM  and  West  Chester  Univer- 
sity, where  she  is  a  Fellow  of  the  Pennsylvania  Writing  Project. 

Ms.  Muzic,  a  native  of  Harrisburg,  is  a  resident  of  South  Williamsport. 

How  would  one  expect  a  respected  English  professor  to  react  to  becoming  the  first  reci- 
pient of  the  College's  Master  Teacher  Award? 

How  about  with  a  fear  of  falling!  Surprisingly,  perhaps,  that  was  the  feeling  described  by 
Ms.  Veronica  M,  Muzic  upon  her  selection  for  the  award  in  1982. 

The  professor,  who  admits  to  a  touch  of  acrophobia  (a  fear  of  high  places),  explained,  "I 
was  seated  at  the  top  of  the  bleachers,  on  stage,  at  commencement  exercises  in  (Bardo)  Gym, 
and,  my  first  thought  was  that  I  would  tumble  down  the  steps  on  way  to  receiving  it." 

She  pressed  lightly  on  the  edge  of  her  desk  and  proceeded  in  an  even  tone.  "My  family," 
she  offered,  "was  visiting  and  1  arrived  home  later  than  expected  for  supper." 

"Following  supper,"  she  recounts  smiling,  "phone  calls  kept  interrupting  a  card  game  we 
were  engaged  in,  (she  feigns  a  frown)  prompting  an  occasional  unceremonious  remark  from  my 
father." 

"The  award  itself,"  she  said,  pouring  a  can  of  pineapple  juice  into  an  ice-filled  cup,  "was 
very  gratifying." 

Teaching  Award  demands  dedication  to  excellence 

Initiated  in  1982,  the  Master  Teacher  Award  is  the  higher  of  two  presentations  (the  other 
being  the  Excellence  in  Teaching  Awards)  offered  annually  at  commencement  exercises  by  the. 
College  as  recognition  for  distinguished  service  in  a  variety  of  areas. 

Eligibility  requires  at  least  three  years  of  successful  service  to  the  College,  but  more  deep- 
ly, it  demands  a  commitment,  in  general,  to  the  enrichment  of  the  entire  College  community. 

Nominations  can  be  initiated  by  any  segment  of  the  College  community  bearing  knowledge 
of  the  nominee's  qualities. 

Now  beginning  her  16th  year  at  the  College,  the  personable  teacher,  committeewoman  and 
women's  rights  advocate  reports  noticing  little  discernible  decline  in  general  student  aptitude 
over  the  years. 

However,  students  have,  she  observed,  altered  their  priorities.  "There  are  obvious 
changes  in  values  and  priorities,"  she  said,  noting  that,  unlike  students  in  the  past,  "today's 
students  tend  to  be  more  concerned  about  jobs  whereas  in  the  past,  attention  seemed  to  focus 
on  intellectual  development." 

"The  institution  itself,"  she  added,  "is  a  marvelous  place  to  work;  as  an  employee  I'm 
given  the  opportunity  to  explore  my  interests." 

Hectic  schedule 

Apart  from  instruction  in  two  specialized  courses  -  Women  in  the  Political  Process  and 
Women  in  Literature  -  the  tireless  rhetorican  instructs  classes  in  Advanced  Composition  and 
English  Composition  II.  The  latter  is  "team  taught"  (a  program  whereby  students  receive  in- 
struction from  more  than  one  individual)  with  colleague  Dr.  Peter  B.  Dumanis  ~  an  experience 
she  terms  "pure  pleasure". 

"I  have  learned  more  than  I  have  taught,"  she  said  modestly,  referring  to  the  team- 
[PLEASE  CONTINUE  READING  ON  NEXT  PAOE] 


Monday,  April  23,  1984     â€˘     SPOTLIGHT     â€˘ 

Involvement/concern;  key  words 

[CONTINUED  FROM  PRECEDING  PAGE] 
teaching  concept. 

Commitment  to  principles  of  involvement  does  not  end,  however,  at  the  academic  level  for 
the  Dauphin  County  native.  The  amicable  professor  is  immersed  in  a  range  of  committee 
memberships  on,  and  beyond,  the  boundaries  of  the  College. 

On  campus,  Ms.  Muzic  serves  on  the  Staff  Development  Committee,  Instructional  Evalua- 
tion Committee,  and  the  International  Education  Committee. 

In  addition  to  having  served  as  past  administrator  of  the  Developmental  Studies  Program 
and  ACT  101  ("COPing")  --  a  program  designed  to  provide  counseling  and  tutorial  services 
for  qualified  students  --  she  currently  serves  as  co-adviser  for  the  Women's  Forum,  a  support 
organization  which  also  sponsors  the  Women's  Week  held  annually  at  the  College. 

Undauted  by  the  rigors  of  these  responsibilities,  Ms.  Muzic  chairs  the  Human  Resources 
Area  Committee  and  is  a  member  of  the  Student  Government  Association  Advisory  Board. 

Further,  she  serves  on  the  ACT  101  Advisory  Board  at  the  College. 

Off  campus,  her  activities  extend  to  participation  in  the  Pennsylvania  Partnership,  in 
association  with  the  Pennsylvania  Commission  for  Women,  which  is  an  organization,  she  ex- 
plains, whose  goal  it  is  to  establish  educational  programs  across  the  state  to  encourage  women 
to  become  more  aware  of  the  political  process  and  to  become  more  involved  in  that  process. 

Additionally,  she  is  on  the  board  of  the  League  of  Women  Voters  in  Lycoming  County 
and  is  presently  serving  on  a  committee  to  assist  in  the  selection  of  a  congressional  page  for  the 
17th  Congressional  District. 

Alluding  to  politics,  Ms.  Muzic  concedes  a  fascination  with  the  political  arena  and  con- 
fesses an  interest  in  perhaps  someday  seeking  political  office  at  a  local  level.  "...But  only", 
she  hastens  to  add,  "if  I  have  the  opportunity  to  effect  good.  I  wouldn't  want  to  be  just  a 
figurehead  or  a  rubberstamp,"  she  said  flatly. 

'Don'l  buy  or  apply  stereotyping' 

Perhaps,  however,  she  is  most  singulariy  intrigued  by  the  quest  of  women  in  this  country 
for  more  equalized  rights.  She  is  an  avid  propronent  of  the  ERA  (Equal  Rights  Amendment)  - 
"essentially  a  matter  of  interpretation".   She  abhors  sexism  in  any  situation. 

"People,"  she  states,  "should  work  up  to  their  own  potential  and  not  be  hamstrung  by 
stereotypical  thinking." 

What  can  women  to  do  improve  their  situation?  "Essentially,"  she  begins  thoughtfully, 
"they  should  become  more  knowledgeable  about  economics  and  the  political  system..." 

A  phone  rings  in  the  office  and,  muttering,  she  moves  to  answer  it. 

Returning,  she  scans  her  desk  and  collects  some  papers.  "Don't  'buy'  or  apply  stereotyp- 
ing to  any  gender,"  she  advises  before  departing.

Did  you  ever  wonder 

who  looks  at  placement  tests? 

Developmental  Studies  staff  does 


By  Donna  M.  Barnelt 
Of  The  SPOTLIGHT  Staff 

Remember  the  placement  test  you  took  before 
you  started  classes  at  the  College?  Did  you  ever 
wonder  who  saw  them  or  how  well  you  did  on  it? 
R.  Dean  Foster,  director  of  developementai  studies 
explained  how  the  results  of  these  tests  are  used. 
"Developmental  courses  are  required  for  some 
students.  The  results  of  the  placement  test  usually 
determines  if  a  student  needs  developmental  courses 
or  not  and  which  courses  he  will  need,"  said 
Foster. 

"If  we  feel  there's  a  mistake  of  some  kind  with 
the  results  --  say  the  student  misnumbered  the  test 
while  he  was  taking  it  -  then  I'll  review  high  school 
transcripts,  SAT's  (Scholastic  Aptitude  Tests)  and 
possibly  re-test  the  student,"  said  Foster. 
Has  three  sections 

Developmental  Studies  is  a  program  designed 
to  help  students  with  deficiencies  in  their  academic 
skills  and  it  is  broken  into  three  sections:  the  Act 
101/COPing  program,  tuloring,and  developmental 
courses,  according  to  Foster. 

The  Act  lOl/COPing  program  was  legislated  in 
1971,  and  was  funded  at  the  College  for  the  first 
time  in  1978-79,  by  the  Higher  Educational  Equal 
Opportunity  Program  to  assist  students  who,  due  to 
poor  performance  on  standardized  tests,  inadequate 
high  school  preparation,  or  both,  would  normally 
not  be  successful,  according  to  information  provid- 
ed by  Foster. 

On  70  campuses 

The  Act  101  program,  known  as  COPing 
"College  Opportunity  Programming  ~  is  active  on 
70  campuses  in  the  state. 

Academic  and  economic  eligibility  re- 
quirements exist  for  those  students  who  take  pail  in 
the  COPing  program.  Academic  eligibility  is  based 
on  placement  test  results.  Any  kind  of  educational 
weakness  can  be  foreseen  as  eligibility  -  a  high 
school  grade  below  80  percent,  the  absence  of  a 
high  school  diploma,  or  a  long  absence  from 
school,  for  example. 

Economic  eligibility  requires  the  student  to 
have  a  family  adjusted  income  below  or  adjusted  to 
$15,000.  Still,  some  students  who  don't  meet  either 
requirement  can  receive  some  COPing  services,  ac- 
cording to  the  information. 

Two  supportive  services  work  in  conjunction 


with  the  COPing  program.  These  are  tutoring  and 
counseling. 

Tutoring  in  the  COPing  program  focuses  on  a 
study  skills  approach  in  content  and  curriculum 
courses.  Counseling  takes  place  in  the  form  of 
group  or  individual  meetings.  This  service  includes 
academic  advising  in  course  selection,  assistance  in 
career  planning  and  personal  financial  counseling. 
James  A.  Bryan  is  the  counselor  for  the 
developmental  studies  program,  according  to 
Foster. 

Alternatives  offered 

A  non-required  summer  program  is  also  of- 
fered to  COPing  students.  Two  alternatives  are  of- 
fered in  the  summer  program.  The  first  choice  in- 
volves the  student  taking  two  developmental  courses 
~  math,  English,  or  reading.  This  session  begins  in 
middle  to  late  June  and  lasts  eight  weeks. 

The  second  alternative  is  for  the  student  to 
enroll  in  a  shorter,  non-credit  program.  This  pro- 
gram lasts  four  weeks. 

For  two  weeks,  students  attend  English, 
reading,  and  math  classes  according  to  the 
developmental  courses  that  are  required  as  a  result 
of  placement  test  results. 

The  students  then  attend  classes  in  their  cur- 
riculum such  as  computer  science,  automotive, 
machine  shop  or  electronics,  according  to  Foster. 
Available  to  all 

The  tutoring  center,  coordinated  by  Ms.  Diana 
L.  Kuhns,  is  located  in  the  Learning  Resources 
Center  and  is  available  to  all  students. 

Tutoring  in  standard  courses  -  English,  math, 
accounting,  physics  and  two  recent  additions  ~ 
chemistry  and  circuit  analysis  is  available, 

Technical  tutoring,  in  which  the  tutor  is 
available  in  each  respective  lab  as  well  as  the  tutor- 
ing center,  includes  a  variety  of  courses:  architec- 
ture, food  and  hospitality,  shorthand,  electronics, 
numerical  control  machinery,  automatic  screw 
machining,  forestry,  plumbing,  computer  science, 
engineering/drafting  and  civil  engineering 
technology,  according  to  Ms.  Kuhns. 
Based  on  test  results 

The  third  section,  developmental  courses,  is 
based  upon  placement  test  results.  Students  enroll- 
ing in  certificate  programs  who  are  deficient  in 

[PLEASE  CONTINUE  READING  ON  NEXT  PAGE] 


Monday,  April  23,  1984     â€˘     SPOTLIGHT     â€˘     27 


ICONTINUED  FROM  PRECEDING  PAGE] 
reading  will  be  required  to  take  a  special  reading 
course,  RDG  010.  This  course,  which  meets  three 
times  a  week,  permits  students  to  gain  skills 
necessary  to  read  textbooks. 

Certificate  program  students  who  have  a 
weakness  in  math  will  schedule  MTH  001.  This 
math  class  gives  instruction  in  the  student  in  basic 
arithmetic,  according  to  Foster. 

Students  enrolled  in  degree  programs  may  be 
required  to  take  ENL  Oil,  MTH  002,  or  RDG  101 
if  their  placement  test  results  are  deficient.  ENL 
Oil  is  a  course  in  basic  writing;  MTH  002,  basic 
and  intermediate  algebra  and  RDG  101  strengthens 
reading  and  study  skills,  said  Foster. 

CHD  (Career  and  Human  Development)  100  is 
required  for  either  certificate  or  degree  progeam 
students  whose  placement  test  shows  a  weakness  in 
any  two  academic  skills.  This  is  an  eight-week 
course  designed  to  help  students  with  career  ex- 
ploration, and  to  improve  decision  making  and 
values  clarification  skills,  he  added. 
Participants  optimistic 
Both  staff  and  students  involved  in  the 
developmental  studies  program  are  very  optimistic 
about  it. 

Luigi  Pellegrino,  aviation  technology  student 
from  South  Williamsport,  said  he  wasn't  too  happy 
about  being  assigned  to  the  program  at  first.  "I 
didn't  like  developmental  studies  at  first.  I  had  too 
many  things  to  do.  Then  I  realized,  it  was 
helping." 

Pellegrino  also  said  he  learned  study  and 
notetaking  with  developemtnal  studies.  It  really 
helps,"  Pellegrino  said.  "You  really  hate  it  all 
times,  but  then  you're  glad  you  did  it." 
'Staff  is  caring' 
Ms.  Karen  M.  Fidler,  floriculture  student  from 
Pine  Grove  saw  her  situation  a  little  differently.  "I 
figured  I'd  have  to  take  developmental  studies 
because  my  spelling  and  reading  weren't  too 
good,"  she  said. 

Ms.  Fidler  also  learned  the  use  of  study  skills 

and  how  to  apply  them  to  different  classes.   "The 

staff  is  caring.  You  just  stop  in  and  they'll  help.  If 

Jim  (counselor  James  A.  Bryan)  sees  you  in  the 

hall,  he'll  stop  and  talk.     1  wouldn't  change 

anything  about  this  program,"  she  said. 

Program  is  'effective' 

"I  figured  it  (developmental  studies)  would 

help  refresh  my  memory  since  it's  been  four  years 

since  I've  been  in  school,"  explained  Ms.  Lisa  E. 

Hoover,  clerical  studies  student  from  Montgomery. 

She  described  the  program  as  "effective"  and 


Did  you  ever  wonder 


"confidence  building"  and  added  "I  wouldn't 
change  anything  (about  the  developmental  studies 
program).  I  couldn't  learn  any  more  in  two 
weeks,"  Ms.  Hoover  said. 

Foster  also  feels  that  the  program  is  effective. 
The  graduation  rate  for  students  in  the  developmen- 
tal studies  program  is  70  percent,  "which  is  as  good 
as  the  College  average,"  according  to  Foster. 
However,  he  feels  he  would  make  only  one  change 
in  the  program.  "I  would  make  developmental 
courses  more  related  to  the  student's  field  of 
study,"  he  said. 

He  also  believes  that  the  Peer  Assistance  Pro- 
gram is  beneficial.  "The  main  job  of  the  peer  assis- 
tant is  to  keep  students  in  touch  with  the 
developmental  studies  program  and  do  some  tutor- 
ing," Foster  said.  "II  provides  a  double  contact 
with  the  students.  Jim  (counselor  James  A.  Bryan) 
sees  most  of  the  students." 

Kenneth  T.  Bader,  computer  science  student 
from  Peckville,  and  Susan  L.  Hughes,  dental 
hygiene  student  from  Williamsport,  are  the  peer 
assistants  for  the  program.

Students  receive  trophies  in  IM  bowling 


Twelve  students  have  won  trophies  for 
special  recognition  as  a  result  of  the  intramural 
bowling  activities. 

These  students  and  other  league  members 
may  pick  up  their  awards  tomorrow  at  4  p.m.  at 
the  ABC  Lanes,  according  to  the  Lanes' 
management. 

Those  who  will  receive  awards  are: 

The  members  of  the  championship  team, 
CU-JO:  Dennis  Rosato,  electrical  occupations 
student  from  Hazlelon;  Dean  D.  Mitchell,  elec- 
trical occupations  student  from  Montrose; 
Thomas  R.  Mears,  electrical  technology  student 
from  Canton,  and  Franklin  J.  Seber,  electrical 
technology  student  from  Scranton. 

The  men's  high  average  award  recipient  for 
the  semester  is  Seber  who  had  a  217  average. 
The  women's  high  average  winner  is  Denise  M. 


King  with  a  143  average. 

The  high  series  winners  are  Mark  E. 
Reamsnyder,  business  management  student  from 
Williamsport,  who  rolled  a  high  741  series  for 
the  men  and  Denise  M.  McNeil,  computer 
science  student  from  Williamsport,  who  tallied  a 
465  high  series  for  the  women. 

The  men's  high  singles  award  belongs  to 
Myles  S.  Murray,  carpentry  construction  student 

from  Wayne,  N.J.,  who  bowled  a  258  game. 

The  women's  high  singles  winner,  Cindy  M. 

Cekovich,     floriculture     student     from 

Mechanicsburg,  rolled  a  199  game. 

The  students  receiving  awards  for  the  most 

improved    average    are    David    C.    Fetzer, 

agribusiness  student  from  Woodward,  and  Lori 

M.    Lane,    journalism    student    from    South 

Williamsport.

Monday,  April  23,  1984 


Summer  programs  to  be  offered  for  children 


The  Center  for  Lifelong  Education  will  offer 
summer  programs  for  children,  according  to 
Mrs.  Carol  F.  Kaufman,  coordinator  of  campus- 
based  activities. 

Programs  in  art,  computer  BASIC,  clown- 
ing, computer  text  editing,  creative  problem 
solving,  photography,  storybook  writing  and  a 
"young  miss"  workshop  among  others  will  be 
offered,  according  to  Mrs.  Kaufman. 

"Not  all  programs  are  offered  for  all 
grades,"  said  Mrs.  Kaufman.  "The  drawing 
class  is  for  sixth  graders  and  up;  the  'young 
miss'  workshop  is  aimed  basically  at  young 
teenagers,  and  the  text-editing  will  be  a  weekly 
course  mainly  for  teenagers." 

Mrs.  Kaufman  also  said  that  an  advanced 


basic  computer  programming  class  will  be  of- 
fered at  the  end  of  the  summer.  "We  ran  five, 
camps  last  summer  for  100  kids  in  basic  com- 
puter programming  alone,"  said  Mrs.  Kaufman. 

"What  we  try  to  do  is  offer  classes  that  the 
student  doesn't  get  in  his  normal  concentration 
in  a  regular  school,"  Mrs.  Kaufman  said. 

People  interested  in  the  one-week  camps 
may  call  Mrs.  Kaufman  in  the  Center  for 
Lifelong  Education  at  326-3727  or  College  Ext. 
231,  she  said.

About  the  Cover 

Delicately  hued  color  photo  by  Mrs.  Greninger  captured  highlights  of  mood 
Photo  was  adjudicated  to  be  first  place  winner  in  Gamma  Epsilon  Tau  contest 

Florence  M.  Greninger  sweeps  photo  contest 

Mrs.  Florence  M.  Greninger,  machine  shop  tool-room  attendant  from  Williamsport,  was 
very  successful  in  winning  the  Gamma  Epsilon  Tau  photo  contest,  according  to  Edward  C. 
Stoddard,  graphic  arts  student  from  Punxsutawney  and  fraternity  president. 

Mrs.  Greninger  not  only  won  the  first  prize,  but  she  also  won  the  second  and  third  prizes 
as  well. 

Harold  L.  Newton,  graphic  arts  instructor  from  Williamsport,  was  selected  for  honorable 
mention  by  the  judges,  said  Stoddard. 

The  judges  met  at  7  p.m.  on  Monday,  April  16,  in  Room  7  of  the  Academic  Center  to 
make  judgement  on  the  photos. 

The  judges  elected  to  place  photos  right  side  up  with  the  names  of  the  contestants  on  the 
backs  and  out  of  view  before  they  started  the  judging.  Without  knowing  the  names  of  the  en- 
trants, judges  placed  a  point  value  on  each  photograph  submitted. 

Stoddard,  who  was  one  of  the  judges  involved  with  the  contest,  said  he  would  also  like  to 
express  a  special  appreciation  to  the  rest  of  the  judges  for  their  cooperation. 

The  other  judges  were  K.  Park  Williams,  production  printer  and  supervisor;  A.  Neale 
Winner,  coordinator  of  instructional  media;  Anthony  N.  Cillo,  associate  professor  of  jour- 
nalism; Miss  Mary  L.  Pease,  journalism  student  from  Blairsville,  and  Frank  A.  Sands,  graphic 
arts  student  from  York.

SGA  seeking  members  for  next  year 

Any  sludent  interested  in  joining  the  Student  Government  Association  (SGA)  for  the 
1984-85  academic  school  year  should  fill  out  the  form  published  on  this  page  and  return  it  to 
the  Intramural  Athletics  Office,  Room  108,  Bardo  Gym  or  the  SGA  Office,  Room  136, 
Academic  Center,  according  to  Steven  D.  Metzker,  business  management  student  from 
Williamsporl,  and  SGA  vice-president. 

Forms  will  then  be  reviewed  by  the  Executive  Committee  and  students  will  be  notiried, 
said  Metzker. 

He  also  added  that  faculty  and  staff  may  recommend  a  student,  but  they  must  complete  a 
form  and  hand  it  in.

New  procedure 
to  be  initiated 
to  aid  students 
in  locating  housing 


Next  semester  there  will  be  a  new  procedure  for  locating 
student  housing,  according  to  Ms.  Mary  M.  Sinibaidi,  ad- 
missions recruiter. 

All  incoming  students  will  receive  a  queslionaire  in 
May.  Once  the  student  completes  the  form  and  returns  it  to 
the  office,  the  information  will  be  placed  on  a  personal  com- 
puter system.   The  students  will  then  receive  a  print-oul  of 
landlords    listing    similar    housing    facilities,    said    Ms. 
Sinibaidi. 
While  the  College  provides  this  service,  she  said,  the  College  does  not  recommend,  ap- 
prove, or  supervise  any  off-campus  rental  properties.  All  agreements  are  between  the  student 
and  the  landlord,  she  said. 

The  Admissions  Office  has  a  booklet,  available  to  students,  entitled  "Facts  About  Hous- 
ing" which  offers  suggestions  and  information  about  locating  appropriate  housing.  The 
booklet  includes  a  checklist  to  be  used  when  reviewing  properties  listed  with  the  College,  Ms. 
Sinibaidi  added. 

Retailer's  seminar  to  be  held  May  9 


On  Wednesday,  May  9,  the  Center  for 
Lifelong  Education,  SCORE  (Service  Corps  of 
Retired  Executives),  and  U.S.  Small  Business 
Administration  will  sponsor  the  Retailer's 
Seminar/Institute,  according  to  Grant  L.  Martin, 
coordinator  of  specialized  technical  educational 
programs. 

Following  a  welcome  by  Louis  S.  Eiseman, 
College  trustee,  the  seminar  will  center  on 
discussions  of  several  needs  of  small  businesses. 

On  the  agenda  are  lectures  on  "How  To 
Apply  For  A  Loan",  by  Theodore  H.  Reich, 
president  of  the  Jersey  Shore  State  Bank; 
"Business  Structures"  by  Gail  G.  Webster,  CPA 
ACE;    "Insurance^  Checklist"    by    Larry    R. 


Young,  vice  president  of  Hartman  Insurance 
Agency;  "Financing  Incentives  for  Development 
of  Your  Business"  by  Kathleen  A.  Steinbacher, 
co-director,  community  and  economic  develop- 
ment for  the  City  of  Williamsport,  and  "Mer- 
chandising and  Inventory  Controls"  by  Robert 
L.  Lyons,  assistant  professor  of  retail  manage- 
ment at  the  College,  according  to  information 
provided  by  Martin. 

PARKING  TICKETS? 
All  parking  tickets  not  setlled  by  this  Fri- 
day, April  27,  will  result  in  a  HOLD  being  plac- 
ed on  grades  and  transcripts.  If  you  have  an 
outstanding  parking  ticket,  go  to  the  Security 
Office  at  the  foot  of  Park  Street.  Office  is  open 
from  8  a.m.  to  4  p.m.  Monday  through  Friday.
